1	# Architecture
2	
3	How the `vc-x1` binary is structured: the clap-aware CLI
4	layer and the (emerging) clap-free subcommand layer.
5	
6	This is a living document; the app is under continuous
7	development and the repo will keep changing. Per-cycle design
8	discussion lives in `notes/chores-*.md`; near-term tasks in
9	`notes/todo.md`.
10	
11	## Overview
12	
13	`vc-x1` is a single binary that supports subcommands,
14	`vc-x1 <subcommand>`, for managing a jj-git **dual-repo workspace**
15	with an app repo plus a `.claude` bot-session repo. The commits
16	cross-reference each other via `ochid:` trailers at the bottom
17	of each commit message. See [`README.md`](README.md)
18	for the user-facing picture and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for
19	the bot workflow.
20	
21	## Two layers: CLI args vs subcommand Context + Params
22	
23	Goal: separate clap-aware argument parsing from subcommand
24	operation logic, so a future front-end (TUI, library
25	embedding) can call the same core without dragging clap
26	along. The trigger was `args.account.account`-style
27	nested-leaf access leaking into subcommand bodies; accessor
28	shortcuts were rejected as hiding the mismatch rather than
29	fixing it.
30	
31	- **CLI layer (clap-aware)**:
32	  Builds `XxxParams` from the user-facing `XxxArgs`
33	  `#[derive(Args)]` clap struct. Options/flags used in more
34	  than one subcommand live as flattened leaves in
35	  `src/options_flags/`.
36	- **Subcommand layer (clap-free)**:
37	  Entrypoint shape: `fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<…>`.
38	  And this isolates the subcommand from user and clap.
39	  - `Context` — common state shared with all subcommands.
40	  - `XxxParams` — a subcommand's parameters, derived from
41	    config or command-line arguments.
42	- **Boundary conversion**:
43	  Converting `XxxArgs` to `XxxParams` uses either
44	  `impl From<&XxxArgs> for XxxParams` or
45	  `impl TryFrom<&XxxArgs> for XxxParams`, depending on whether
46	  the conversion can fail.
47	
48	Two parameters per subcommand; the signature documents what
49	the subcommand depends on. `Context` is the same across all
50	subcommands; `XxxParams` carry the per-subcommand inputs.
51	
52	Rules for the subcommand layer (some still aspirational — see
53	[Migration A](#migration-a--args--context--params)):
54	
55	1. Plain options, flat fields, `Default` — no clap types in
56	   `XxxParams`. Domain types (`RepoSelector`, `Scope`) are
57	   fine; leaf wrappers (`RepoOption`) are not.
58	2. Typed errors (`enum XError`, not `Box<dyn Error>`) — a
59	   TUI matches variants to pick dialogs; CLI formats them.
60	3. Returned outcomes, not `println!` — each subcommand
61	   returns a structured result; CLI formats it, a library
62	   writes nothing to stdout itself.
63	4. Progress via an optional `&mut dyn ProgressSink` on
64	   long-running subcommands — CLI installs a stderr sink, tests a
65	   recording sink.
66	5. No globals, no implicit cwd/env reads in subcommands — CLI
67	   resolves cwd/env once at startup and builds `Context`.
68	
69	None of this is sacred — the whole structure is an experiment
70	and the repo keeps changing. The current scope is deliberately
71	narrow:
72	
73	- a small `Context` — two params, not one god blob;
74	- no trait-based DI yet;
75	- one crate, not a workspace split;
76	- `src/options_flags/` leaves left in place — only their
77	  consumers change shape.
78	
79	Widen any of it when the evidence (a second front-end, a second
80	consumer crate, a real platform-surface divergence) calls for
81	it — not before.
82	
83	### Naming
84	
85	Earlier drafts of this design (and the `notes/chores-09.md`
86	capture) used `Workspace` / `XOptions`. The implementation
87	(0.44.0) chose `Context` / `XxxParams` instead:
88	
89	- `Workspace` → `Context` — Cargo owns "workspace" formally,
90	  and this codebase already uses "workspace" for the
91	  dual-repo project root (`find_workspace_root`).
92	- `XOptions` → `XxxParams` — avoids visual collision with
93	  `Option<T>` (a params struct mixes required and
94	  `Option<T>`-typed fields) and with `src/options_flags/`.
95	
96	## Module map
97	
98	CLI layer:
99	
100	- `src/main.rs` — the clap edge:
101	  - `pub struct Cli` — clap `Parser`.
102	  - `pub enum Commands` — clap `Subcommand`; matched in the
103	    dispatch arms.
104	  - `cli_with_banner()` — walks the command tree adding the
105	    `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as `before_help` on every subcommand.
106	  - `CompleteEnv` wiring for dynamic completion.
107	  - `bm_track` — a permanent `main`-bookmark tracking
108	    diagnostic printed on entry/exit of every command.
109	
110	- `src/options_flags/` — options/flags shared across
111	  subcommands, so a flag is defined once (parser, help,
112	  completer); Migration B tracks what's been moved here so
113	  far. See
114	  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md).
115	  - reusable **leaves**: `account`, `config`, `dry_run`,
116	    `private`, `push_retry`, `repo`, `scope`, `squash`,
117	    `use_template`.
118	  - reusable **bundles**: `provision_bundle`.
119	- `src/common.rs` — `CommonArgs` (the shared positional-rev /
120	  `-R` repo-list / `-n` / `--limit` / `-L` flag set flattened
121	  by `chid`, `desc`, `list`, `show`) plus the repo-iteration
122	  and revision-resolution helpers they share.
123	
124	Subcommand layer scaffolding:
125	
126	- `src/context.rs` — `Context`: the shared platform handle
127	  every subcommand runs against. Today it carries the loaded
128	  `UserConfig` and the `--log` path; built once at startup
129	  via `Context::load(log)`.
130	- `src/config.rs` — `UserConfig` (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`).
131	- `src/scope.rs` — `Scope` enum (`Roles(Vec<Side>)` for the
132	  dual-repo `code`/`bot` roles, `Single(PathBuf)` for
133	  single-repo mode) and `parse_scope`.
134	
135	Subcommand modules — each holds an `XxxArgs` `#[derive(Args)]`
136	struct and a `pub fn x(...)` entrypoint:
137	
138	| Module | Subcommand | Notes |
139	| --- | --- | --- |
140	| `chid.rs` | `chid` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
141	| `desc.rs` (+ `desc_helpers.rs`) | `desc` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
142	| `list.rs` | `list` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
143	| `show.rs` | `show` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
144	| `validate_desc.rs` | `validate-desc` | migrated (0.48.0-4) — `validate_desc(&Context, &ValidateDescParams)`, `From<&ValidateDescArgs>` |
145	| `fix_desc.rs` | `fix-desc` | migrated (0.48.0-5) — `fix_desc(&Context, &FixDescParams)`, `From<&FixDescArgs>` |
146	| `clone.rs` | `clone` | migrated (0.48.0-2) — `clone_repo(&Context, &CloneParams)`, `From<&CloneArgs>` |
147	| `init.rs` (+ `init/params.rs`) | `init` | subcommand-layer worked example (0.44.0) |
148	| `symlink.rs` | `symlink` | migrated (0.48.0-1) — `symlink(&Context, &SymlinkParams)`, `From<&SymlinkArgs>` |
149	| `sync.rs` | `sync` | migrated (0.48.0-3) — `sync(&Context, &SyncParams)`, `From<&SyncArgs>` |
150	| `finalize.rs` | `finalize` | migrated (0.46.0) — `finalize(&Context, &FinalizeParams)`, `TryFrom<&FinalizeArgs>` |
151	| `push.rs` | `push` | resumable state machine; migrated (0.48.0-6) — `push(&Context, &PushParams)`, `From<&PushArgs>` |
152	
153	Support:
154	
155	- `src/repo_utils.rs`, `src/url.rs`, `src/toml_simple.rs`,
156	  `src/logging.rs` — jj/repo helpers, URL parsing, a minimal
157	  TOML reader, the CLI logger.
158	- `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` —
159	  `#[cfg(test)]` fixtures (`Fixture` / `FixturePor`) and
160	  tempdir resolution. Subcommand modules also carry their
161	  own `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (and `integration_tests` for
162	  `sync` / `push`).
163	
164	## Subcommand model
165	
166	Adding a subcommand `x`:
167	
168	1. Create `src/x.rs` with `pub struct XxxArgs`
169	   (`#[derive(Args)]`), composing leaves from
170	   `src/options_flags/` where one exists.
171	2. Add a variant `X(x::XxxArgs)` to `Commands` in
172	   `src/main.rs` with a `///` summary (and
173	   `#[command(long_about = …)]` for the long form).
174	3. Add a dispatch arm in `main()` calling `x::x(&args)` (or,
175	   once ported, `x::x(&ctx, &params)`).
176	4. Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive `--help`; add
177	   `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, …)]` on any field whose doc
178	   comment uses bullets (clap otherwise reflows them into
179	   prose).
180	5. Dynamic value completion (`--account=<TAB>`) is an
181	   `ArgValueCompleter` attached to the leaf in
182	   `src/options_flags/` — completion is a clap-aware concern
183	   and stays at this layer.
184	
185	## Migration A — args → Context + Params
186	
187	Port each subcommand's `pub fn x(args: &XxxArgs)` to
188	`pub fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams)`, adding an
189	`XxxParams` flat struct + a `From` (or `TryFrom`, if the
190	conversion is fallible) at the binary edge. `init` (0.44.0) is
191	the worked example; `finalize` (0.46.0) added the `TryFrom` /
192	`Context.log` variant; the `0.48.0` cycle swept the rest of the
193	standalone subcommands. Only `chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`
194	remain — their Migration A is bundled into the future
195	"CommonArgs sweep" (Migration A + B are entangled for the
196	`CommonArgs`-flattening subcommands). The live checklist is the
197	"Subcommand layer / CLI decoupling" item in
198	[`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md).
199	
200	| Subcommand | Status |
201	| --- | --- |
202	| `init` | done (0.44.0) — `init(&Context, &InitParams)`; `From<&InitArgs>` |
203	| `finalize` | done (0.46.0) — `finalize(&Context, &FinalizeParams)`; `TryFrom<&FinalizeArgs>` (fallible boundary); `--log` moved onto `Context` |
204	| `symlink` | done (0.48.0-1) — `symlink(&Context, &SymlinkParams)`; `From<&SymlinkArgs>` |
205	| `clone` | done (0.48.0-2) — `clone_repo(&Context, &CloneParams)`; `From<&CloneArgs>` |
206	| `sync` | done (0.48.0-3) — `sync(&Context, &SyncParams)`; `From<&SyncArgs>` |
207	| `validate-desc` | done (0.48.0-4) — `validate_desc(&Context, &ValidateDescParams)`; `From<&ValidateDescArgs>` |
208	| `fix-desc` | done (0.48.0-5) — `fix_desc(&Context, &FixDescParams)`; `From<&FixDescArgs>` |
209	| `push` | done (0.48.0-6) — `push(&Context, &PushParams)`; `From<&PushArgs>` (collapses the two bookmark spellings) |
210	| `chid` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
211	| `desc` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
212	| `list` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
213	| `show` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
214	
215	Out of scope for these ports (deferred until a real consumer
216	surfaces): typed errors, returned outcomes vs `println!`, the
217	`ProgressSink`, and `Context` fields beyond `UserConfig` + the
218	`--log` path (`finalize` surfaced `log`).
219	
220	## Migration B — per-subcommand flags → `src/options_flags/`
221	
222	Independently of Migration A, per-subcommand inline `#[arg]`
223	fields are being lifted into reusable leaves/bundles under
224	`src/options_flags/` so a flag is defined (parser, help,
225	completer) exactly once. See
226	[`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
227	for the leaf / bundle / Pattern-A mechanics and the
228	Flag-vs-Option classification.
229	
230	State today:
231	
232	- **`init`** — fully composed from leaves/bundles
233	  (`account`, `repo`, `scope`, the `provision` bundle,
234	  `use_template`, `config`).
235	- **`chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`** — share
236	  `common::CommonArgs`, a per-domain shared struct rather
237	  than an `options_flags/` leaf. The "CommonArgs sweep"
238	  todo item folds these into the leaf model (and drops the
239	  repeatable `-R`/`--repo` in favor of the `--scope` path
240	  form).
241	- **`finalize`** — `--squash` lifted to the `squash` leaf
242	  (which carries the `value`-field naming convention — see
243	  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
244	  "Adding a new leaf"). `--delay` / `--detach` / `--exec` /
245	  `--repo` / `--push` stay inline (no second consumer yet).
246	- **`sync` / `push` / `clone` / `validate-desc` /
247	  `fix-desc` / `symlink`** — still mostly inline `#[arg]`
248	  fields. The `--scope` retrofits queued in
249	  [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) are the usual entry
250	  point for converting one.
251	
252	## See also
253	
254	- [`README.md`](README.md) — user-facing overview and
255	  per-subcommand usage.
256	- [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) — bot workflow, versioning,
257	  commit/push conventions, code conventions.
258	- [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
259	  — leaf/bundle patterns for Migration B.
260	- [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) — live task list,
261	  including both migrations.
262	- [`notes/chores-09.md`](notes/chores-09.md) — the
263	  0.43.0 / 0.44.0 design capture this document supersedes.

# Architecture

How the `vc-x1` binary is structured: the clap-aware CLI
layer and the (emerging) clap-free subcommand layer.

This is a living document; the app is under continuous
development and the repo will keep changing. Per-cycle design
discussion lives in `notes/chores-*.md`; near-term tasks in
`notes/todo.md`.

## Overview

`vc-x1` is a single binary that supports subcommands,
`vc-x1 <subcommand>`, for managing a jj-git **dual-repo workspace**
with an app repo plus a `.claude` bot-session repo. The commits
cross-reference each other via `ochid:` trailers at the bottom
of each commit message. See [`README.md`](README.md)
for the user-facing picture and [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for
the bot workflow.

## Two layers: CLI args vs subcommand Context + Params

Goal: separate clap-aware argument parsing from subcommand
operation logic, so a future front-end (TUI, library
embedding) can call the same core without dragging clap
along. The trigger was `args.account.account`-style
nested-leaf access leaking into subcommand bodies; accessor
shortcuts were rejected as hiding the mismatch rather than
fixing it.

- **CLI layer (clap-aware)**:
  Builds `XxxParams` from the user-facing `XxxArgs`
  `#[derive(Args)]` clap struct. Options/flags used in more
  than one subcommand live as flattened leaves in
  `src/options_flags/`.
- **Subcommand layer (clap-free)**:
  Entrypoint shape: `fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams) -> Result<…>`.
  And this isolates the subcommand from user and clap.
  - `Context` — common state shared with all subcommands.
  - `XxxParams` — a subcommand's parameters, derived from
    config or command-line arguments.
- **Boundary conversion**:
  Converting `XxxArgs` to `XxxParams` uses either
  `impl From<&XxxArgs> for XxxParams` or
  `impl TryFrom<&XxxArgs> for XxxParams`, depending on whether
  the conversion can fail.

Two parameters per subcommand; the signature documents what
the subcommand depends on. `Context` is the same across all
subcommands; `XxxParams` carry the per-subcommand inputs.

Rules for the subcommand layer (some still aspirational — see
[Migration A](#migration-a--args--context--params)):

1. Plain options, flat fields, `Default` — no clap types in
   `XxxParams`. Domain types (`RepoSelector`, `Scope`) are
   fine; leaf wrappers (`RepoOption`) are not.
2. Typed errors (`enum XError`, not `Box<dyn Error>`) — a
   TUI matches variants to pick dialogs; CLI formats them.
3. Returned outcomes, not `println!` — each subcommand
   returns a structured result; CLI formats it, a library
   writes nothing to stdout itself.
4. Progress via an optional `&mut dyn ProgressSink` on
   long-running subcommands — CLI installs a stderr sink, tests a
   recording sink.
5. No globals, no implicit cwd/env reads in subcommands — CLI
   resolves cwd/env once at startup and builds `Context`.

None of this is sacred — the whole structure is an experiment
and the repo keeps changing. The current scope is deliberately
narrow:

- a small `Context` — two params, not one god blob;
- no trait-based DI yet;
- one crate, not a workspace split;
- `src/options_flags/` leaves left in place — only their
  consumers change shape.

Widen any of it when the evidence (a second front-end, a second
consumer crate, a real platform-surface divergence) calls for
it — not before.

### Naming

Earlier drafts of this design (and the `notes/chores-09.md`
capture) used `Workspace` / `XOptions`. The implementation
(0.44.0) chose `Context` / `XxxParams` instead:

- `Workspace` → `Context` — Cargo owns "workspace" formally,
  and this codebase already uses "workspace" for the
  dual-repo project root (`find_workspace_root`).
- `XOptions` → `XxxParams` — avoids visual collision with
  `Option<T>` (a params struct mixes required and
  `Option<T>`-typed fields) and with `src/options_flags/`.

## Module map

CLI layer:

- `src/main.rs` — the clap edge:
  - `pub struct Cli` — clap `Parser`.
  - `pub enum Commands` — clap `Subcommand`; matched in the
    dispatch arms.
  - `cli_with_banner()` — walks the command tree adding the
    `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as `before_help` on every subcommand.
  - `CompleteEnv` wiring for dynamic completion.
  - `bm_track` — a permanent `main`-bookmark tracking
    diagnostic printed on entry/exit of every command.

- `src/options_flags/` — options/flags shared across
  subcommands, so a flag is defined once (parser, help,
  completer); Migration B tracks what's been moved here so
  far. See
  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md).
  - reusable **leaves**: `account`, `config`, `dry_run`,
    `private`, `push_retry`, `repo`, `scope`, `squash`,
    `use_template`.
  - reusable **bundles**: `provision_bundle`.
- `src/common.rs` — `CommonArgs` (the shared positional-rev /
  `-R` repo-list / `-n` / `--limit` / `-L` flag set flattened
  by `chid`, `desc`, `list`, `show`) plus the repo-iteration
  and revision-resolution helpers they share.

Subcommand layer scaffolding:

- `src/context.rs` — `Context`: the shared platform handle
  every subcommand runs against. Today it carries the loaded
  `UserConfig` and the `--log` path; built once at startup
  via `Context::load(log)`.
- `src/config.rs` — `UserConfig` (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`).
- `src/scope.rs` — `Scope` enum (`Roles(Vec<Side>)` for the
  dual-repo `code`/`bot` roles, `Single(PathBuf)` for
  single-repo mode) and `parse_scope`.

Subcommand modules — each holds an `XxxArgs` `#[derive(Args)]`
struct and a `pub fn x(...)` entrypoint:

| Module | Subcommand | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `chid.rs` | `chid` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
| `desc.rs` (+ `desc_helpers.rs`) | `desc` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
| `list.rs` | `list` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
| `show.rs` | `show` | flattens `CommonArgs` |
| `validate_desc.rs` | `validate-desc` | migrated (0.48.0-4) — `validate_desc(&Context, &ValidateDescParams)`, `From<&ValidateDescArgs>` |
| `fix_desc.rs` | `fix-desc` | migrated (0.48.0-5) — `fix_desc(&Context, &FixDescParams)`, `From<&FixDescArgs>` |
| `clone.rs` | `clone` | migrated (0.48.0-2) — `clone_repo(&Context, &CloneParams)`, `From<&CloneArgs>` |
| `init.rs` (+ `init/params.rs`) | `init` | subcommand-layer worked example (0.44.0) |
| `symlink.rs` | `symlink` | migrated (0.48.0-1) — `symlink(&Context, &SymlinkParams)`, `From<&SymlinkArgs>` |
| `sync.rs` | `sync` | migrated (0.48.0-3) — `sync(&Context, &SyncParams)`, `From<&SyncArgs>` |
| `finalize.rs` | `finalize` | migrated (0.46.0) — `finalize(&Context, &FinalizeParams)`, `TryFrom<&FinalizeArgs>` |
| `push.rs` | `push` | resumable state machine; migrated (0.48.0-6) — `push(&Context, &PushParams)`, `From<&PushArgs>` |

Support:

- `src/repo_utils.rs`, `src/url.rs`, `src/toml_simple.rs`,
  `src/logging.rs` — jj/repo helpers, URL parsing, a minimal
  TOML reader, the CLI logger.
- `src/test_helpers.rs`, `src/test_tmp_root.rs` —
  `#[cfg(test)]` fixtures (`Fixture` / `FixturePor`) and
  tempdir resolution. Subcommand modules also carry their
  own `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (and `integration_tests` for
  `sync` / `push`).

## Subcommand model

Adding a subcommand `x`:

1. Create `src/x.rs` with `pub struct XxxArgs`
   (`#[derive(Args)]`), composing leaves from
   `src/options_flags/` where one exists.
2. Add a variant `X(x::XxxArgs)` to `Commands` in
   `src/main.rs` with a `///` summary (and
   `#[command(long_about = …)]` for the long form).
3. Add a dispatch arm in `main()` calling `x::x(&args)` (or,
   once ported, `x::x(&ctx, &params)`).
4. Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive `--help`; add
   `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, …)]` on any field whose doc
   comment uses bullets (clap otherwise reflows them into
   prose).
5. Dynamic value completion (`--account=<TAB>`) is an
   `ArgValueCompleter` attached to the leaf in
   `src/options_flags/` — completion is a clap-aware concern
   and stays at this layer.

## Migration A — args → Context + Params

Port each subcommand's `pub fn x(args: &XxxArgs)` to
`pub fn x(ctx: &Context, params: &XxxParams)`, adding an
`XxxParams` flat struct + a `From` (or `TryFrom`, if the
conversion is fallible) at the binary edge. `init` (0.44.0) is
the worked example; `finalize` (0.46.0) added the `TryFrom` /
`Context.log` variant; the `0.48.0` cycle swept the rest of the
standalone subcommands. Only `chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`
remain — their Migration A is bundled into the future
"CommonArgs sweep" (Migration A + B are entangled for the
`CommonArgs`-flattening subcommands). The live checklist is the
"Subcommand layer / CLI decoupling" item in
[`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md).

| Subcommand | Status |
| --- | --- |
| `init` | done (0.44.0) — `init(&Context, &InitParams)`; `From<&InitArgs>` |
| `finalize` | done (0.46.0) — `finalize(&Context, &FinalizeParams)`; `TryFrom<&FinalizeArgs>` (fallible boundary); `--log` moved onto `Context` |
| `symlink` | done (0.48.0-1) — `symlink(&Context, &SymlinkParams)`; `From<&SymlinkArgs>` |
| `clone` | done (0.48.0-2) — `clone_repo(&Context, &CloneParams)`; `From<&CloneArgs>` |
| `sync` | done (0.48.0-3) — `sync(&Context, &SyncParams)`; `From<&SyncArgs>` |
| `validate-desc` | done (0.48.0-4) — `validate_desc(&Context, &ValidateDescParams)`; `From<&ValidateDescArgs>` |
| `fix-desc` | done (0.48.0-5) — `fix_desc(&Context, &FixDescParams)`; `From<&FixDescArgs>` |
| `push` | done (0.48.0-6) — `push(&Context, &PushParams)`; `From<&PushArgs>` (collapses the two bookmark spellings) |
| `chid` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
| `desc` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
| `list` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |
| `show` | deferred — rides the CommonArgs sweep (Migration A + B entangled) |

Out of scope for these ports (deferred until a real consumer
surfaces): typed errors, returned outcomes vs `println!`, the
`ProgressSink`, and `Context` fields beyond `UserConfig` + the
`--log` path (`finalize` surfaced `log`).

## Migration B — per-subcommand flags → `src/options_flags/`

Independently of Migration A, per-subcommand inline `#[arg]`
fields are being lifted into reusable leaves/bundles under
`src/options_flags/` so a flag is defined (parser, help,
completer) exactly once. See
[`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
for the leaf / bundle / Pattern-A mechanics and the
Flag-vs-Option classification.

State today:

- **`init`** — fully composed from leaves/bundles
  (`account`, `repo`, `scope`, the `provision` bundle,
  `use_template`, `config`).
- **`chid` / `desc` / `list` / `show`** — share
  `common::CommonArgs`, a per-domain shared struct rather
  than an `options_flags/` leaf. The "CommonArgs sweep"
  todo item folds these into the leaf model (and drops the
  repeatable `-R`/`--repo` in favor of the `--scope` path
  form).
- **`finalize`** — `--squash` lifted to the `squash` leaf
  (which carries the `value`-field naming convention — see
  [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
  "Adding a new leaf"). `--delay` / `--detach` / `--exec` /
  `--repo` / `--push` stay inline (no second consumer yet).
- **`sync` / `push` / `clone` / `validate-desc` /
  `fix-desc` / `symlink`** — still mostly inline `#[arg]`
  fields. The `--scope` retrofits queued in
  [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) are the usual entry
  point for converting one.

## See also

- [`README.md`](README.md) — user-facing overview and
  per-subcommand usage.
- [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) — bot workflow, versioning,
  commit/push conventions, code conventions.
- [`src/options_flags/README.md`](src/options_flags/README.md)
  — leaf/bundle patterns for Migration B.
- [`notes/todo.md`](notes/todo.md) — live task list,
  including both migrations.
- [`notes/chores-09.md`](notes/chores-09.md) — the
  0.43.0 / 0.44.0 design capture this document supersedes.

1	# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags
2	
3	Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
4	shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
5	type, parser, and tests stay together.
6	
7	This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for "Migration B" in
8	[`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md); see there for
9	how the leaves fit the wider clap-args / `Context`+`Params`
10	split.
11	
12	## Architecture
13	
14	Three composition patterns:
15	
16	- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
17	  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
18	  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
19	  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
20	  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
21	  consumer.
22	- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` that flattens N leaves into a
23	  named role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle`). One
24	  `#[command(flatten)]` line at the consumer picks up the whole
25	  bundle.
26	- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
27	  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
28	  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
29	  leaf's typed value and parser.
30	
31	## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax
32	
33	Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
34	domain it carries:
35	
36	- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
37	  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
38	  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
39	  name suffix: `*Flag`.
40	- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
41	  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
42	  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
43	  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).
44	
45	Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.
46	
47	## Adding a new leaf
48	
49	1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
50	   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
51	2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
52	   (non-boolean).
53	3. Name the field(s):
54	   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
55	     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
56	     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
57	     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
58	     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
59	   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
60	     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
61	     flag from its field name as usual.
62	   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
63	     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
64	     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
65	     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
66	     [`../../notes/todo.md`](../../notes/todo.md).
67	4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
68	5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
69	   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
70	   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
71	   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
72	   handles them.
73	6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
74	7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.
75	
76	## Consuming an OF
77	
78	Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:
79	
80	```rust
81	#[derive(Args)]
82	pub struct MyArgs {
83	    #[command(flatten)]
84	    pub config: ConfigOption,
85	    // ...
86	}
87	```
88	
89	Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:
90	
91	```rust
92	#[derive(Args)]
93	pub struct MyArgs {
94	    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
95	    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
96	          verbatim_doc_comment)]
97	    pub config: Option<String>,
98	    // ...
99	}
100	```
101	
102	A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
103	and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
104	attributes.
105	
106	## Consumer function shape
107	
108	Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
109	relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
110	fields at the call site:
111	
112	```rust
113	// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
114	fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
115	             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }
116	
117	run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
118	```
119	
120	This wins on:
121	- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
122	  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
123	  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
124	  choice).
125	- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
126	  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
127	  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
128	- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
129	  zero-touch change at every call site.
130	
131	For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
132	`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
133	(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
134	pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
135	`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.
136	
137	## Marker traits
138	
139	Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
140	implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
141	(their category lives in the type-name suffix).
142	
143	- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
144	  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
145	- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
146	  constituent leaf is an Option).
147	- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
148	  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.
149	
150	Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
151	a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):
152	
153	- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
154	  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
155	  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
156	- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
157	  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
158	  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.
159	
160	`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
161	the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
162	targets).
163	
164	All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
165	see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
166	the appropriate ones explicitly.
167	
168	## Bundle marker discipline
169	
170	Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
171	bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
172	an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
173	(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
174	drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
175	bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.
176	
177	## Pattern A worked example
178	
179	`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
180	("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
181	`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
182	surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
183	becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
184	worked example.
185	
186	## Layout note
187	
188	OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
189	README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
190	(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
191	in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
192	OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
193	to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
194	`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
195	not done preemptively.

# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags

Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
type, parser, and tests stay together.

This module is the CLI-layer leaf store for "Migration B" in
[`../../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../../ARCHITECTURE.md); see there for
how the leaves fit the wider clap-args / `Context`+`Params`
split.

## Architecture

Three composition patterns:

- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
  consumer.
- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` that flattens N leaves into a
  named role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle`). One
  `#[command(flatten)]` line at the consumer picks up the whole
  bundle.
- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
  leaf's typed value and parser.

## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax

Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
domain it carries:

- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
  name suffix: `*Flag`.
- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).

Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.

## Adding a new leaf

1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
   (non-boolean).
3. Name the field(s):
   - **Single-field leaf** — call the field `value` (it holds the
     parsed value-side of the option) and declare the flag
     explicitly with `#[arg(long = "<flag>", …)]`. The consumer
     then reads `args.<leaf>.value` rather than doubling the flag
     name (`args.squash.value`, not `args.squash.squash`).
   - **Multi-field leaf** — descriptive per-field names
     (`push_retries`, `push_retry_delay`); clap derives each
     flag from its field name as usual.
   - The pre-existing single-field leaves (`scope`, `repo`,
     `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template`)
     predate this and still derive the flag from the field name;
     migrating them to `value` is tracked in
     [`../../notes/todo.md`](../../notes/todo.md).
4. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
5. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
   handles them.
6. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
7. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.

## Consuming an OF

Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    #[command(flatten)]
    pub config: ConfigOption,
    // ...
}
```

Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
          verbatim_doc_comment)]
    pub config: Option<String>,
    // ...
}
```

A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
attributes.

## Consumer function shape

Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
fields at the call site:

```rust
// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }

run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
```

This wins on:
- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
  choice).
- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
  zero-touch change at every call site.

For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `SquashOption`, `DryRunFlag`,
`PrivateFlag`), direct read at the consumer site
(`args.squash.value`, or `args.provision.dry_run.dry_run` for the
pre-`value` leaves) is fine — wrapping the inner value in a
`&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn the indirection.

## Marker traits

Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
(their category lives in the type-name suffix).

- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is an Option).
- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.

Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):

- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.

`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
targets).

All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
the appropriate ones explicitly.

## Bundle marker discipline

Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.

## Pattern A worked example

`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
worked example.

## Layout note

OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
not done preemptively.

content.text:
Read through the Migration B notes, the `options_flags/` README, and the current leaf set. Here's my read on it:

## They overlap, but they're not the same operation

- **The "single-field `value`" sweep (todo #2, queued as a `0.47.1` candidate)** is a *rename within the leaves that already live in `options_flags/`* — `scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template` get their field renamed to `value`, plus the handful of consumers (`init.rs`, `test_helpers.rs`, the `From<&InitArgs>` impl). It's cosmetic-uniformity work: make all leaves match the convention `squash` introduced in 0.47.0.
- **Migration B** is a *structural* move: lift inline `#[arg]` fields out of a subcommand and into a new shared leaf/bundle. Different inputs, different risk, different review.

So "related" — yes, both land in `options_flags/` — but I'd treat them as a **sequence, not a merge**: do the `value` rename *first* as its own small cycle, then do Migration B work on top. Reasons:

1. **The rename is a precondition.** If you do Migration B first, every new leaf you add follows the `value` convention and sits next to seven old leaves that don't — you've widened the inconsistency. Renaming first means the bar is uniform before you add to the pile.
2. **Keeps diffs legible.** Bundling a mechanical 7-leaf rename into a structural sweep makes the structural diff noisy and the review harder. Separate commits, separate concerns.
3. **The rename is genuinely small now** — and *this* is where your "now that all subcommands have `XxxArgs`/`XxxParams`" observation pays off: post-Migration-A, the leaf-field reads mostly live inside the `From<&XxxArgs>` impls rather than scattered through subcommand bodies, so both sweeps touch fewer sites than they would have a few cycles ago. Good time for both — just not in one commit.

## On "move all options/flags to options_flags now"

I'd push back on the *blanket* version. The established discipline (ARCHITECTURE.md "Migration B", and the 0.47.0 finalize note explicitly) is **lift a flag when it has a real second consumer, not speculatively** — `finalize` deliberately kept `--delay`/`--detach`/`--exec`/`--repo`/`--push` inline because nothing else uses them. A sweep that moves every inline flag into `options_flags/` produces a bunch of single-consumer leaves, which contradicts the repo's stated "widen when the evidence calls for it" stance. And the `CommonArgs` family (`chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show`) is explicitly entangled with the `--scope` retrofit, so it can't be a pure mechanical move regardless.

What's *actually* shared right now and a legit Migration B candidate (real ≥2 consumers):
- `--bookmark` — `finalize`, `push`, `sync` all have one.
- the `--repo` / `--other-repo` / `--id-len` trio — `validate-desc` and `fix-desc` share it (note: this is a *path*, semantically different from the existing `RepoOption` which is a `RepoSelector`, so it'd be a new leaf/bundle, not a reuse).
- `--yes` — `symlink`, `push`, others.
- broaden `DryRunFlag` adoption — `clone` and the `*-desc` pair have their own dry-run handling.

And the todo already says the `--scope` retrofit cycles (`push --scope`, `clone --scope`, `validate-desc/fix-desc --scope`) are "the usual entry point for converting one" — i.e. the intended path is *Migration B rides each `--scope` retrofit*, one subcommand per cycle, rather than one big sweep.

## Suggestion

1. `0.49.0` — the `value`-field rename sweep (todo #2). Small, mechanical, makes the leaf convention uniform. Single-step.
2. After that, pick *either* path for Migration B:
   - **Incremental, riding `--scope`** (matches the todo's intent): each `--scope` retrofit cycle also converts that subcommand's flag set to leaves.
   - **One focused Migration-B cycle** for the genuinely-shared-now flags only (`--bookmark`, the `*-desc` repo trio, `--yes`) — explicitly *not* a blanket move.

Want me to draft a plan for the `value`-field sweep as `0.49.0`, or do you want to talk through the Migration B scoping (incremental-vs-focused) first?

Currently discussing how — I recommended doing the small "value"-field rename sweep first as 0.49.0, then Migration B incrementally. Next: you decide whether I draft that 0.49.0 plan or we scope Migration B first. (disable recaps in /config)

lastPrompt: Got it, that was much clearer, I think we should have series tasks; --scope, --bookmark, *-desc repo trio, --yes, and DryRunFlag plus anything else I missed so we complete Migration A + B as a series…

aiTitle: Move options and flags to src/options_flags

content.text:
Good framing — these genuinely cluster. One distinction worth drawing first, though, because it changes the size estimate a lot:

**Two flavors are getting lumped together.**
- **(a) Consolidation** — the `value`-field rename, lifting shared inline flags into leaves, converting `CommonArgs` to the leaf model. Mechanical, no behavior change. *This* is "finish Migration B" (+ the `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` Migration A, which rides along).
- **(b) `--scope` rollout** — `push`/`clone`/`finalize`/`*-desc` gaining scope-awareness. These are *features* with behavior change — `push`'s state machine going scope-aware (single-side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/`finalize-claude`) is the big one. And note: `push`/`clone`/`finalize` are *already* done for Migration A **and** B-as-far-as-it-goes — adding `--scope` to them isn't migration work at all, it's the separate "`--scope` sweep" line item.

The only place (a) and (b) fuse unavoidably is the **CommonArgs sweep**: you can't convert `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` to Context+Params without also turning `CommonArgs` into leaves *and* swapping the repeatable `-R` for the `--scope` path form — todo even flags them "entangled." So that one step is (a)+(b) by necessity; the rest of (b) is separable.

## Inventory (yours + what I found)

Migration B / consolidation candidates with real ≥2 consumers today:
- **`value`-field rename** — `scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`, `account`, `config`, `use_template` + consumers (`init.rs`, `test_helpers.rs`, the `From` impls). Precondition for everything else (new `--scope` consumers want `args.scope.value`).
- **`--bookmark` leaf** — `sync` (required `String`), `finalize` (`Option<String>`, spelled `--push`), `push` (`Option<String>`, two spellings). Folding this is also the natural home for the push dual-bookmark collapse ([89]) and the `--recheck` decide-or-remove ([90]).
- **`*-desc` trio leaf/bundle** — `--repo` (a `PathBuf` here, *not* the `RepoSelector` of the existing `RepoOption`, *not* the repeatable `Vec<PathBuf>` of `CommonArgs` — three different `--repo` semantics in the tree, watch out) + `--other-repo` + `--id-len`. Shared by `validate-desc`/`fix-desc`.
- **`DryRunFlag` broadening** — `clone` (`--dry-run`), `fix-desc` (`--no-dry-run`), `push` (`--dry-run`); `init` already uses the leaf via `provision_bundle`.
- **`--yes` leaf** — `symlink`, `push` (and `finalize`? it doesn't have one — only `--detach`/`--exec`). Two consumers, borderline-worth-it.
- **`--remote`** — `sync` only (and `bm_track` internals). *Not* shared; leave inline.
- **CommonArgs → leaf model** — the `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` four; completes Migration A.

`--scope` rollout (the (b) pile, separable except CommonArgs): `push --scope`, `clone --scope`, `finalize --scope` (replacing `--repo`), `validate-desc`/`fix-desc --scope`.

Out of scope (tangential, don't conscript): `WorkspaceConfig` typed-struct unification, `--check`/`--no-check` mirrored onto `push`, the help-layout over-under fix.

## Proposed shape

I'd **split it**, recommending:

**`0.49.0-N` — "complete Migration B + finish Migration A":**

| Step | Content |
| --- | --- |
| `-0` | plan + version bump + chores section + todo ladder |
| `-1` | `value`-field rename sweep (7 leaves + consumers) |
| `-2` | `DryRunFlag` broaden → `clone` / `fix-desc` / `push` |
| `-3` | `--yes` leaf → `symlink` / `push` |
| `-4` | `--bookmark` leaf → `sync` / `finalize` / `push`; absorb push dual-bookmark collapse [89] + `--recheck` decision [90] |
| `-5` | `*-desc` trio leaf/bundle → `validate-desc` / `fix-desc` |
| `-6` | CommonArgs sweep — `CommonArgs` → leaves + `--scope` form (drops `-R`); port `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` to Context+Params. **Completes Migration A.** Likely needs `-6.N` sub-steps. |
| `0.49.0` | close-out — drop suffix, flip both ARCHITECTURE.md migration tables to all-done, todo→Done, chores final |

**`0.50.0-N` — "`--scope` rollout":** `clone --scope`, `finalize --scope` (replace `--repo`), `validate-desc`/`fix-desc --scope`, `push --scope` (scope-aware state machine — biggest, may need sub-steps).

**Alternative:** one mega `0.49.0-N` with all of it. Feasible under the substep protocol — every `-N` lands committed-and-reviewable independently — but it'll run across many sessions, and the project's own history warns about that (the 0.42.0 close-out note: closing a sprawling cycle for "narrative coherence" beats stretching it). If you want one cycle, I'd at least keep `push --scope` as the *last* step so the cycle can close cleanly before it if it balloons.

## Caveats

- **`init-clone-refactor` merge ordering.** The `icr` branch is still unmerged (todo: "decide merge direction"), and a `clone`-touching step on `main` makes a future `icr` rebase messier — `clone --scope` was *originally* `0.42.0-6`, deferred partly for this reason. If `clone --scope` lands in `0.50.0-N`, decide the `icr` merge direction *first*. (The `0.49.0-N` ladder above doesn't touch `clone` except the `DryRunFlag` flatten in `-2`, which is tiny — but flag it.)
- **`finalize --scope` replacing `--repo`** is a breaking CLI change with a migration story; that's its own design conversation when `0.50.0` gets there, not a mechanical retrofit.

Want me to draft the `0.49.0-0` plan against the split shape (the table above)? Or do you want the mega-cycle, or to adjust the ladder first?

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by

branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions

content.text:
Surveyed it. Here's what's stale and what I'd capture — nothing edited yet, want your go-ahead first.

## icr cleanup — stale references found

`init-clone-refactor` was rebased *into* `main` at `0.42.0-4.7` (chores-09 documents it: "Rebased main … onto init-clone-refactor at its 0.41.1 close-out tip"), and you've now deleted the branch local+remote. So:

| Location | Current state | Proposed fix |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `notes/todo.md` ~L92 — "Decide merge direction for `init-clone-refactor` [78]" | The decision was made (main rebased onto icr at -4.7) | **Delete** the item |
| `notes/todo.md` ~L96 — "Cross-file chores-NN ordering sanity pass at `init-clone-refactor` merge time … Tied to merge-direction decision above [78]" | Merge happened; chores-08.md is on `main` already | **Reword** to a standalone low-pri "chores-NN cross-file ordering sanity pass" (drop the icr/merge-time framing) — or delete if you'd rather. Your call |
| `notes/todo.md` ~L56 — "Rebase note — CLAUDE.md `### Per-file review checkpoints` … When 0.42.0 rebases on top of 0.41.1 …" | That rebase happened; CLAUDE.md's `### Per-file review checkpoints` reads coherently | **Delete** — appears resolved by -4.7 |
| `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` — "Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)" + the "open decisions" section + the L215 pointer to those todo items | Predates the -4.7 landing; reads as if the branch is still live and decisions still open | Add a closing **"## Status as of 2026-05-11"** note: landed into `main` at 0.42.0-4.7, branch deleted local+remote, the open decisions below are resolved/moot — retained as historical post-mortem. (No rewrite of the dated sections — keeps the record honest.) |

Left alone (just illustrative, not "indicates unmerged"): `CLAUDE.md` L446/L637 use `init-clone-refactor` as an *example* feature-branch name; `notes/forks-multi-user.md` uses it in hypothetical `vc-x1 push --code-bookmark` snippets; `notes/done.md` / `chores-07,08` entries are historical records. Say the word if you want the CLAUDE.md examples swapped to a neutral placeholder (`feature-x`) too.

## por/dual goal — capture

Goal as I read it: **por and dual should be first-class equals** (today dual is the primary path, por is bolted on), and add **bidirectional conversion** — `por → dual` (attach a `.claude` companion + `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo) and `dual → por` (detach/drop the companion). Open questions to record, not resolve now: new subcommand(s) (`vc-x1 convert`? `promote`/`demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands; what happens to the `.claude` history on `dual → por`; how `.vc-config.toml` is created/removed; whether `clone`/`init` change.

Where I'd put it:
- A new **`notes/por-dual-parity.md`** stub — short, same forward-looking-capture shape as `forks-multi-user.md` / `bot-data-formats.md`: goal statement, the two sub-goals (parity; bidirectional conversion), the open questions above, an explicit "pre-design — lands when a cycle picks it up."
- A **`notes/todo.md`** item near the top of `## Todo` ("por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion") with a `[N]` ref pointing at that stub. It naturally heads the `--scope` cluster — the scope rollout is the substrate this builds on.

## Packaging

I'd do this as a small **`0.48.1`** docs-only cycle — bump `Cargo.toml` 0.48.0 → 0.48.1, the notes edits above, one commit + push. Matches the `0.42.1` precedent ("documentation-only follow-on to the close-out") exactly, and keeps `0.49.0-0` purely the Migration B/A opener. Alternative: fold the icr cleanup into `0.49.0-0` and the por/dual stub into `0.49.0-0` too — fewer commits, but the por/dual goal is a non-sequitur in a Migration-B commit.

One sequencing check: after `0.48.1`, is it `0.49.0-N` (Migration B completion) next, then `0.50.0-N` (scope) — or do you want to jump straight to scope? You said "we'll focus on scope" after the housekeeping; want to make sure I've got the order right.

Confirm: (1) `0.48.1` micro-cycle ok? (2) reword vs. delete the chores-ordering todo item? (3) new `notes/por-dual-parity.md` stub ok, or capture it as just a todo item? (4) 0.49.0 then 0.50.0, or scope next?

Next: you answer the four confirm questions. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
=== notes/README.md ===
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

App architecture — module map, the CLI-args / `Context`+`Params`
split, the subcommand model, and the in-flight migrations — lives
at the repo root in [`../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ARCHITECTURE.md).

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.
