input.content:
# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion

Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).

`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
on. Two goals:

## 1. Parity

por and dual should be first-class equals across every
subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.

## 2. Bidirectional conversion

A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
shapes in place.

- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
  cross-links going forward.
- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.

## Open questions (resolve at design time)

- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
  `dual → por`.
- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
  interrupted mid-way.

## See also

- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions taken
(see Resolution below).

## Resolution (2026-05-11)

`init-clone-refactor` landed into `main` at `0.42.0-4.7`
(see `notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`): `main`'s `0.42.0-0..-4.6` work was
rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
branch has since been deleted locally and remotely.

The "still deferred" / "still open" wording in the dated
sections below is superseded — the merge-direction question
is resolved (rebase-main-onto-branch), and the chores-NN
ordering follow-up survives only as a low-priority entry in
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo`. This file is retained as a
historical post-mortem; the recovery playbook and
diagnostic techniques remain reusable.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

Merge direction and chores-NN ordering moved to
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (top entries) for
discoverability. Remaining technical question:

- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

-  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will

   its field `value`, declares the flag via `#[arg(long = "…")]`,
   so consumers read `args.<leaf>.value` not `args.<leaf>.<leaf>`)
   on the new `squash` leaf. Sweep the pre-existing single-field
   leaves to match: `scope`, `repo`, `dry_run`, `private`,
   `account`, `config`, `use_template` + their consumers
   (`init.rs`, tests). Multi-field leaves (`push_retry`) keep
   descriptive names. Candidate for `0.47.1`. [87]
1. **por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion.** Make
   `por` (Plain Old Repo — single repo, no `.claude/`, no
   `.vc-config.toml`) and `dual` (code repo + `.claude` bot
   session) first-class equals — today dual is the primary
   path and por is bolted on. Then add conversion both ways:
   `por → dual` (attach a `.claude` companion +
   `.vc-config.toml`) and `dual → por` (detach the
   companion). Builds on the `--scope` rollout (items
   below). Pre-design; open questions in [91]. [91]

+# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion
+
+Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
+lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
+`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).
+
+`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
+plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
+trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
+shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
+`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
+on. Two goals:
+
+## 1. Parity
+
+por and dual should be first-class equals across every
+subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
+"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
+rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
+`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
+substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.
+
+## 2. Bidirectional conversion
+
+A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
+shapes in place.
+
+- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
+  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
+  cross-links going forward.
+- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
+  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.
+
+## Open questions (resolve at design time)
+
+- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
+  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
+- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
+  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
+  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
+- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
+  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
+  `dual → por`.
+- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
+  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
+- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
+  interrupted mid-way.
+
+## See also
+
+- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
+  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
+- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
+  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
+- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
+  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
+  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

+# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion
+
+Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
+lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
+`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).
+
+`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
+plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
+trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
+shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
+`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
+on. Two goals:
+
+## 1. Parity
+
+por and dual should be first-class equals across every
+subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
+"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
+rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
+`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
+substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.
+
+## 2. Bidirectional conversion
+
+A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
+shapes in place.
+
+- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
+  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
+  cross-links going forward.
+- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
+  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.
+
+## Open questions (resolve at design time)
+
+- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
+  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
+- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
+  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
+  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
+- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
+  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
+  `dual → por`.
+- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
+  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
+- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
+  interrupted mid-way.
+
+## See also
+
+- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
+  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
+- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
+  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
+- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
+  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
+  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

content.text:
You're right, and the principle's correct — `por-dual-parity.md` is the source of truth; everything else should *point*, not *copy*. Let me sharpen which of those `rg` hits are actually a problem, though, because they're not all the same:

- **`todo.md` line 45 — real duplication.** The `## Todo` item is a 10-line paragraph that re-states the stub's content. The project's own todo-format rule (`notes/README.md` / CLAUDE.md: "Detailed motivation … belong in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.") says this should be a terse pointer. **Fix: trim it.**
- **`init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` line 239 — soft dup.** It quotes the todo item's *title* verbatim. Not content duplication, but unnecessary — it can just say "a low-priority entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`". **Minor tweak.**
- **`chores-09.md` lines 979 / 991 / 995 — not duplication.** That subsection is the *record of what the 0.48.1 commit did*; naming the artifacts it touched ("new `## Todo` item 'por/dual parity…'", "reworded the 'Cross-file chores-NN…' item") is exactly an Edits list's job. The *phrase* recurring there is unavoidable and correct — it's describing, not defining. **Leave it.**
- **`todo.md` line 247 (`## Done`) — just a title.** Fine.

So: one real fix (trim the todo item), one cosmetic (drop the quoted title in the icr doc), plus I'll lowercase the `por-dual-parity.md` H1 to match how it's written everywhere else (`# por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion`). Applying now:

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions taken
(see Resolution below).

## Resolution (2026-05-11)

`init-clone-refactor` landed into `main` at `0.42.0-4.7`
(see `notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`): `main`'s `0.42.0-0..-4.6` work was
rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
branch has since been deleted locally and remotely.

The "still deferred" / "still open" wording in the dated
sections below is superseded — the merge-direction question
is resolved (rebase-main-onto-branch), and the chores-NN
ordering follow-up survives only as a low-priority entry in
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo`. This file is retained as a
historical post-mortem; the recovery playbook and
diagnostic techniques remain reusable.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (resolved 2026-05-11)

The merge-direction call, once the recovered branch
gave a clean base to choose from:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Resolved: the "vice versa" topology — `main`'s
`0.42.0-0..-4.6` work rebased on top of the branch at
its `0.41.1` close-out tip, force-pushed at close-out
(`0.42.0-4.7`). See Resolution above and
`notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions (resolved — see Resolution above)

All resolved by the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase landing:

- **Merge direction** — settled: `main`'s `0.42.0-X`
  work rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1`
  close-out tip.
- **chores-NN ordering** — survives only as a
  low-priority "Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering
  sanity pass" entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0** — the real
  conflicts (`Cargo.toml`, `notes/todo.md`,
  `notes/chores-08.md`, CLAUDE.md, `src/init.rs`,
  `src/scope.rs`, `src/common.rs`) surfaced and were
  resolved during the cascade; see
  `notes/chores-09.md > ### What needed real work`.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

# por/dual Parity + Bidirectional Conversion

Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).

`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
on. Two goals:

## 1. Parity

por and dual should be first-class equals across every
subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.

## 2. Bidirectional conversion

A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
shapes in place.

- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
  cross-links going forward.
- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.

## Open questions (resolve at design time)

- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
  `dual → por`.
- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
  interrupted mid-way.

## See also

- `notes/todo.md` — the "por/dual parity + bidirectional
  conversion" item and the `--scope` cluster it builds on.
- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.

+## Open questions (resolved — see Resolution above)
+All resolved by the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase landing:
+- **Merge direction** — settled: `main`'s `0.42.0-X`
+  work rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1`
+  close-out tip.
+- **chores-NN ordering** — survives only as a
+  low-priority entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
+- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0** — the real
+  conflicts (`Cargo.toml`, `notes/todo.md`,
+  `notes/chores-08.md`, CLAUDE.md, `src/init.rs`,
+  `src/scope.rs`, `src/common.rs`) surfaced and were
+  resolved during the cascade; see
+  `notes/chores-09.md > ### What needed real work`.
+# por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion
+
+Forward-looking design capture (pre-design). Concrete work
+lands when a cycle picks it up — likely on top of the
+`--scope` rollout (the `0.50.0` work in `notes/todo.md`).
+
+`vc-x1` today treats the **dual** workspace (a code repo
+plus a `.claude` bot-session repo, cross-linked by `ochid:`
+trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) as the primary
+shape; **por** (Plain Old Repo — a single repo, no
+`.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`) is supported but bolted
+on. Two goals:
+
+## 1. Parity
+
+por and dual should be first-class equals across every
+subcommand — same code paths, same quality of support, no
+"dual plus an afterthought" asymmetry. The `--scope`
+rollout (`code,bot` vs `por` — see `notes/todo.md` and
+`src/options_flags/scope.rs`'s `ScopeKind`) is the
+substrate; parity is the goal that rollout serves.
+
+## 2. Bidirectional conversion
+
+A new capability: convert a workspace between the two
+shapes in place.
+
+- **`por → dual`** — attach a `.claude` companion repo and
+  a `.vc-config.toml` to an existing single repo; emit
+  cross-links going forward.
+- **`dual → por`** — detach the `.claude` companion; drop
+  `.vc-config.toml`; the code repo continues standalone.
+
+## Open questions (resolve at design time)
+
+- Surface: a new subcommand (`vc-x1 convert`?
+  `promote` / `demote`?) vs. flags on existing commands.
+- `dual → por` — what happens to the `.claude` history:
+  archived, left in place untouched, deleted? Leading
+  guess is "left alone, just unlinked," but TBD.
+- `.vc-config.toml` — creation on `por → dual` (which
+  fields, defaults from user config?) and removal on
+  `dual → por`.
+- Whether `init` / `clone` change, or conversion is purely
+  a separate operation on an already-existing workspace.
+- Idempotency / re-running; recovery from a conversion
+  interrupted mid-way.
+
+## See also
+
+- `notes/todo.md` — the por/dual item, and the `--scope`
+  cluster it builds on.
+- `src/options_flags/scope.rs` — `ScopeKind` (`CodeBot` /
+  `Por`) and `parse_scope_kind`.
+- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
+  — the `init_one` / `clone_one` primitives that aim to
+  give a single chokepoint for scope-shaped changes.
+1. **por/dual parity + bidirectional conversion.** Make
+   `por` and `dual` first-class equals (dual is primary
+   today, por bolted on); add `por → dual` / `dual → por`
+   conversion. Builds on the `--scope` rollout below.
+   Pre-design; goal + open questions in the stub. [91]
+1. Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering sanity pass.
+   `chores-08.md` (the 0.41.1 cycle) landed on `main` via
+   the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase; check that section ordering
+   across `chores-06`/`-07`/`-08`/`-09` is chronologically
+   coherent and normalize if not. Low priority.

- **`notes/por-dual-parity.md`** — the single source of truth for the *goal* (parity, `por → dual` / `dual → por`, open questions). H1 lowercased to match the topic phrasing.
- **`notes/todo.md`** — the por/dual `## Todo` item is now a 5-line pointer (was 10): names the task, says it builds on the `--scope` rollout, defers the substance to `[91]`. (`## Done` entry and the chores `[92]` ref unchanged.)
- **`notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`** — the "Open questions (resolved)" bullet no longer quotes the todo item's title, just points at `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
- **`notes/chores-09.md`** — left as-is on purpose: that subsection is the *record of the 0.48.1 commit*, so naming the artifacts it touched ("new `## Todo` item …", "reworded the 'Cross-file chores-NN …' item") is the Edits-list's job, not content duplication.

The remaining "Cross-file `chores-NN.md` ordering" hits: the only one with *substance* is the `## Todo` item itself (its source of truth); the icr-doc and chores-09 references just point at / name it. The `forks-multi-user.md` and `chores-06.md` "cross-file" hits are unrelated (different context).

message.content:
No need for `# Edits` section in `chores-xx.md` that information will be in the
commit message and it should be the source of truth. Two suggestions:
1) The section title in a chores-xx.md file should exactly match the name of the commit.
   Of course the initial section title will probably be wrong, but at the time the
   commit is created one last edit is made and the section title is updated along
   with every reference to it!
2) When starting another change for the same repo grab the commits link, such as:
   https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904
   and update the chores-xx.md section to have that link in a list of one or more references
   to the commits, something like ; `Commits: [23],[25]`

Thoughts

content.text:
Strong yes on the direction. The principle I'd state it as: **git is the source of truth for "what changed mechanically"; `chores-NN.md` is the source of truth for "why, and the design thinking"; the two are cross-linked, neither restates the other.** Today's setup has the *commit body* mirror the chores `### Edits` list — same content, two places, and the duplication CLAUDE.md acknowledges but resolves the wrong way (it names the markdown the truth; the immutable, tooled, naturally-commit-scoped record is the better home). Your three points fix exactly that. Per-point:

**1) Drop `### Edits` from `chores-NN.md` — yes.** What stays in a chores section: the intro paragraph (now pitched a notch higher — "this step ports X to Context, no behavior change" rather than a file list), the `###` design subsections (the durable rationale a commit message is the wrong place for), and the new `Commits:` pointer. The one cost: to see "which files," a markdown reader takes one hop (click the commit link) instead of reading inline — acceptable, since CLAUDE.md already requires the commit *body* to carry the curated "file + one-line gist" list, so nothing is lost, it just lives in the one place that describes that commit. (`.claude`-side commits are unaffected — they never had the dup.)

**2) Chores heading === commit title, synced at finalize — yes, with eyes open.** Real costs:
- The heading inherits the commit-title form: `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, ≤50 chars. So `## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)` replaces today's freer `## push → Context + PushParams (0.48.0-6)`. The descriptive richness (arrow notation, type names) moves into the intro paragraph; the heading's job becomes *identify*, not *describe*.
- "Update every reference to it" = every `[N]: /notes/chores-NN.md#anchor` back-ref (in `todo.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, sibling chores files, the file itself) gets re-pointed when the heading changes. That's the "one last edit." It needs to be a pre-commit-checklist item, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should check anchor integrity (dangling `#anchor` refs are the failure mode).
- Timing slots into the existing flow cleanly: do the work → draft the chores section with a provisional heading → at the message-approval gate, settle the final title → edit the chores heading + back-refs to match → `vc-x1 push <bm> --title "<that title>" --body "<the edit list>"`. The chores edit rides in the same commit it describes.

**3) `Commits: [N],[M]` ref list with the commit URL — yes.** Caveats:
- **One-commit lag.** The URL doesn't exist until `push-app` runs, so it gets backfilled in the *next* cycle's commit (your "when starting another change, grab the link" step). Accept it — the commit itself is the truth; the `Commits:` line is a browse-convenience, and a markdown reader who hits a not-yet-backfilled section just runs `git log --grep "(0.49.0-3)"`. Make "backfill the previous section's `Commits:` ref" a cycle-start checklist item. (Future: `vc-x1 push` could do the backfill itself right after `push-app`, since the SHA is known then — but that's a forward-looking enhancement, not a blocker.)
- **Host coupling.** `https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<sha>` rots if the repo moves. The project's already drifting toward GitHub URLs for refs (there's a todo for `blob/main/...` source refs), so it's consistent — but consider pairing it with the short SHA in the ref text so `git show <sha>` works host-independently: e.g. `[23]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c… (e284505c)`. Your call.
- Placement: a `Commits: [23]` line as the first line under the heading (metadata-style, immediately visible); the `[23]: <url>` target in the file's bottom `# References` section, using the existing machinery (`[23],[25]`, not `[23][25]`).

**Open sub-decisions before this lands:**
- **Retroactive scope.** Backfilling `Commits:` refs and renaming headings across `chores-01..08` is a lot of churn for little value — I'd say apply going forward only, maybe do `chores-09` as the worked example, leave 01–08 with their `### Edits` lists (harmless). Your call.
- **Squash interaction.** When a multi-step cycle lands *squashed to one commit*, do the per-substep subsections each point `Commits:` at that one squashed SHA (5 subsections → same SHA), or collapse the subsections too? Needs a rule.

**Scoping/sequencing.** This is a CLAUDE.md + `notes/README.md` convention change — it shouldn't ride the `0.49.0` code cycle. Options:
- **(a)** Amend it into the in-flight `0.48.1` (not pushed yet) — `0.48.1` becomes "icr cleanup + por/dual capture + chores convention," and itself drops its `### Edits` / renames its heading as the worked example. Fewest commits, but grows `0.48.1`'s scope.
- **(b)** Keep `0.48.1` as-is (reviewed, tight), do `0.48.2` next purely for the convention change — which forward-edits `0.48.1`'s chores subsection to the new shape as the demonstration. *My lean* — keeps each commit single-purpose.
- **(c)** Fold into `0.49.0-0` so the Migration B cycle's commits use the new convention from step one.

Which scoping ((a)/(b)/(c)), and your calls on retroactive scope + the squash rule? I'll write the CLAUDE.md/notes-README edits once you pick.

message.content:
- 0.48.2, I like how we concluded 0.48.1 but as I said the "host coupling"
situation is important and you said:
"but consider pairing it with the short SHA in the ref
text so git show <sha> works host-independently"

I don't quite understand how a short SHA helps as the
full SHA is unchanging independent of the host. Do you
mean settle on the first 12 characters as the short
SHA like we do with ochids?

Or maybe you mean github doesn't require the full SHA
and it turns out a shorter one works so this 10 character
SHA https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c34
works?

I just logged into my gitlab.com account for the first time
in 6 years and they support short SHA too:
https://gitlab.com/winksaville/kademlia-dht-rs/-/commit/7b233696cec.
Of course the syntax is slightly different,  then domain, obviously
but gitlab also look to have an "/-/" between "<repo-name> and "commit".

Clarifications, Thoughts?

content.text:
Yeah — I conflated two separate things in that earlier line. Let me untangle them.

**"Host coupling" is purely about the URL *prefix*, not the SHA.** The SHA (`e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904`) is content-addressed git data — it's identical in every clone regardless of where `origin` points. What rots is the wrapper:
- GitHub: `https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/commit/<sha>`
- GitLab: `https://gitlab.com/<owner>/<repo>/-/commit/<sha>` ← your observation; GitLab inserts `/-/` to disambiguate project paths from sub-resources (it supports nested groups)
- self-hosted Gitea/cgit/etc.: different again

So if the project ever moves hosts, every `https://github.com/...` ref in the notes is dead. The SHA inside it isn't — `git show <sha>` still works. *That's* all I meant: don't bake only the host-specific URL into the durable record; keep the host-agnostic SHA as the real identifier. The "short SHA" remark was a tangent and a muddled one — sorry.

**Your two questions, answered:**
1. *GitHub/GitLab accepting short SHAs in URLs* — yes, both do. `https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c34` works and GitHub 302-redirects to the canonical full URL; GitLab same. 7 chars (git's old default) works too. So a short SHA in a URL is fine *today*.
2. *"first 12 chars like ochids"* — that'd be a reasonable short convention, but note the namespaces differ: ochids are **jj change IDs** (`qqkonsnsvkqo` — base-32-ish, 12 chars by `--id-len` default), while a `Commits:` ref points at the **git commit SHA** (hex, what GitHub indexes). So it'd be an *analogy* to the ochid convention, not a reuse of it. 12 hex chars is plenty collision-safe for this repo's lifetime (48 bits; you'd need ~tens of millions of commits before it's a real risk).

**The catch with any short SHA:** a hard-coded abbreviation that's unambiguous *today* can become ambiguous as the repo grows — `git show e284505c` will eventually refuse if another object shares that prefix. The full 40-char SHA never has that problem. So short = prettier but has a (distant, theoretical) expiry; full = bulletproof and ugly.

**What I'd recommend for the `0.48.2` convention** — and this is the bit you flagged for more discussion, so push back freely:

Store the **bare full git SHA** as the `[N]:` ref target:
```
[23]: e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904
```
Rationale: it's the git-native pointer, host-agnostic, never ambiguous, `git show <sha>` follows it in any clone — and it's *consistent with the whole premise* of this change ("git is the source of truth; the markdown points, doesn't restate"). A URL is a presentation-layer convenience; baking it into the notes pulls a host dependency into the durable record.

Trade-off I won't hide: a bare SHA in a rendered `.md` on GitHub is **not** auto-linkified (GitHub auto-links SHAs in commit messages / issues / PR text, but not in repo markdown files, as far as I know — worth a 30-second test before we commit to it). So you lose one-click browsing; you'd paste the SHA into the URL bar or `git show` it.

If that clickability matters enough, the defensible alternatives:
- **SHA + URL, SHA first as the canonical part:** `[23]: e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904 — https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/e284505c34` — belt-and-suspenders; if the host changes you fix the parenthetical, the SHA stays. Verbose but unambiguous about which half is truth.
- **URL only, full SHA in it:** `https://github.com/.../commit/e284505c34...e49229252904` — clickable now, host-coupled, SHA still hand-extractable from a dead link. Closest to the project's existing direction (there's a todo to adopt `blob/main/...` GitHub URLs for source refs).

My vote: **bare full SHA**. Second choice: **SHA + URL (SHA first)**. The pure-URL form I'd avoid for the durable record.

Want me to (a) lock one of these, or (b) do a quick test of GitHub markdown SHA auto-linking first, then decide — before I draft the `0.48.2` CLAUDE.md edits?

content.text:
Two things — first a syntax wrinkle that bends the "full SHA first" idea slightly, then the `0.48.2` plan.

## The `Commits:` ref format

For `[N]` to *render as a clickable link* (which it does in this project — `[78]` etc. are reference-style links), the `[N]: …` definition has to put the **URL as the destination** — markdown reference-link defs are `[label]: <destination> "<optional title>"`, and `[N]` links to the destination. So we can't literally put the SHA first in the definition without breaking the click.

But the **title slot** gets us everything you asked for:

```
Commits: [1]
…
[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/3f176b45235c "3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e"
```

(`3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e` is the actual `0.48.1` commit; `3f176b45235c` is its first 12 hex.)

- `[1]` → clickable → GitHub/GitLab resolve the 12-char prefix → commit page. ✓ surf-and-click
- The full 40-char SHA sits in the `"…"` — visible in the raw `.md`, so an external DB scraping the notes gets the canonical, host-agnostic identifier (and `git show 3f176b…` works in any clone). ✓ external docs / database
- URL stays short. ✓
- Host moves to GitLab/self-hosted? The full SHA in the title survives; you rewrite the destination URL (and yes, GitLab needs the `/-/` you spotted). The *truth* never depended on the host. ✓
- Uses the existing `[N]` machinery; `Commits: [1],[3]` for multi-commit. ✓

The only concession: the URL is *syntactically* first in the definition (markdown requires it); the full SHA rides in the title. But it's still right there in the source, which is what the DB/external-docs case actually needs. (The alternative — an inline `[<full-sha>](<url>)` so the SHA is the visible link text — works but bloats the section body and drops out of `# References`; I'd not.)

OK with this form? If so:

## `0.48.2` plan (single-step)

- **`Cargo.toml`**: `0.48.1` → `0.48.2`.
- **`CLAUDE.md`**:
  - `### Chores section headers` — the chores section heading is now **exactly the commit title** (`<type>: <desc> (<version>)`); set provisionally during the work, synced to the final title (and every anchor back-ref to it updated) as the last edit before `vc-x1 push`. Descriptive richness moves to the intro paragraph.
  - `### Chores: scannable bullets…` — **drop the `### Edits` subsection**. The commit message body is the source of truth for the file-by-file list. A chores section keeps: the `Commits:` line (first line under the heading), the intro paragraph, and the `###` design subsections.
  - New text — the `Commits: [N],[M]` convention: points at the git commit(s) the section describes; ref def is `[N]: <url-with-12-char-SHA> "<full-40-char-SHA>"`; backfilled when the *next* change to that repo starts (the latest section is briefly `Commits:`-less — fine, the commit itself is the record).
  - The squash interaction: a squash-to-one-commit close-out now also owns the heading/`Commits:`/anchor fixups across every squashed-away section — real work, so the bias tips further toward "keep separate."
  - `### App-repo body` — body bullets *are* the canonical change list now (not "mirrors chores"); each = file + one-line gist.
  - `### Pre-commit checklist` — reword item 8; add "sync chores heading → final commit title + fix anchor back-refs"; add a cycle-start item "backfill the previous chores section's `Commits:` ref."
- **`notes/README.md`**: note that chores `# References` now also hold commit refs in the `[N]: <url> "<full-sha>"` form.
- **`notes/chores-09.md`** (the worked example): add a `# References` section (file-local, starts `[1]`); reshape the `## por-dual parity capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1)` section → rename to `## docs: por-dual capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1)` (its real commit title), drop its `### Edits`, add `Commits: [1]` → the SHA above; **then** add the `0.48.2` section *born in the new shape* (intro + a `###` subsection capturing this convention, no `### Edits`, `Commits:` line backfilled later in `0.49.0-0`). So the file ends with one fully-converted section + one freshly-minted one — both states demonstrated.
- **`notes/todo.md`**: re-point `[92]` to the renamed 0.48.1 anchor; add the `0.48.2` `## Done` entry + its `[93]` ref (re-pointed at push time when the 0.48.2 heading/anchor settles — the convention in action).

Single-step (one commit + push). It's a few docs files but one coherent change. Approve the ref format + this plan and I'll bump to `0.48.2`, write the plan into the new-shape chores-09 section, and start implementing (per-file review as usual)?

### Chores section headers

A chores section header is **exactly the commit title** it
records — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)):

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
```

The header is provisional while the work is in progress; the
*last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it to the final commit
title — and updates every markdown anchor back-reference to it
(`[N]: /notes/chores-NN.md#the-old-anchor` lines in `todo.md`,
`ARCHITECTURE.md`, sibling chores files, the file itself; see
[Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links) for the slug
algorithm). A dangling `#anchor` is the failure mode — the
pre-commit checklist catches it, and a future `vc-x1
validate-repo` should too.

Existing sections (most of chores-01..09) predate this and keep
their old `## Description (X.Y.Z)` headers; the convention
applies going forward — `0.48.2` converts the `0.48.1` section
as the worked example.

input.old_string:
### Chores: scannable bullets, detail in subsections

A per-step chores subsection opens with a short intro, then a
`-` bullet list that mirrors the commit body — one line per
file/edit, a scannable index. When a bullet starts wanting to
explain *why* something is the way it is, or to flag a wrinkle,
don't inflate the bullet:

- Promote the explanation to its own `###` subsection inside the
  same `chores-NN.md`; point the bullet at it with a markdown
  anchor link (`[gist](#the-subsection-heading)`).
- If the wrinkle is a live design concern — something that
  *should* change, not just be recorded — also add a
  `notes/todo.md` item, with a `[N]` ref pointing at that
  subsection (todo→chores is the normal ref direction).

**Why:** verbose bullets bury the index, and detail written
twice (bullet + subsection) drifts. One source of truth, linked
from wherever it's relevant.
input.new_string:
### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
design subsections. It does **not** carry a per-file edit list —
that lives in the commit message body, which is the source of
truth for "what changed mechanically" (immutable, `git
show`-able, naturally scoped to the commit). The chores section
is the source of truth for the design thinking; the two
cross-link, neither restates the other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [3]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering));
multiple commits are `Commits: [3],[5]`. The `# References`
definition puts the **commit URL** as the destination (so `[N]`
renders as a clickable link), with the **full 40-hex SHA** in
the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

input.old_string:
### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
9. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)
input.new_string:
### Pre-commit checklist

At cycle *start* (before the version bump): backfill the
previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
commit's URL + full SHA — see
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references).

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
   body).
9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

### Pre-commit checklist

At cycle *start* (before the version bump): backfill the
previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
commit's URL + full SHA — see
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references).

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo fmt`
2. `cargo clippy`
3. `cargo test`
4. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required: without it, `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock`
   and re-resolves from scratch, which can pick incompatible
   versions even when `cargo build` / `cargo test` succeed.
5. Retest after install
6. Update `notes/todo.md` — for multi-step cycles, flip the
   just-completed step's marker from `(current)` to `(done)`
   **before** running `vc-x1 push`. The commit being pushed
   should reflect the new state. (The next step's `(current)`
   marker is set later, at the *start* of that step — see the
   Versioning multi-step section.)
7. Update `notes/todo.md` — at cycle close-out (final commit),
   move the entry from `## In Progress` to `## Done`.
8. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a section (header =
   provisional commit title; intro paragraph + any `###` design
   subsections; **no** per-file edit list — that's the commit
   body).
9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

input.old_string:
Two valid shapes for landing the sub-step stack on `main`:

- **Squash to one cycle commit** (default; matches the prior
  `0.41.1-6.0`–`-6.6` pattern). Single entry on `main`;
  sub-step granularity preserved only in the commit body's
  edit list. Right when the work is one logical change with
  intermediate validation points.
- **Keep separate (N + 1 commits)** when the decomposition is
  itself informative (different conceptual stages, design
  progression worth showing in `git log`). Used on
  `0.41.1-6.7` (8 sub-sub-step commits + 1 close-out commit).

Pick at close-out, not at cycle start. Default is squash;
deviate when the artifact is the decomposition.
input.new_string:
Two valid shapes for landing the sub-step stack on `main`:

- **Squash to one cycle commit** — single entry on `main`;
  sub-step granularity preserved only in the commit body's
  edit list. Right when the work is one logical change with
  intermediate validation points. **Cost:** the per-sub-step
  chores sections collapse into one, whose header becomes the
  single close-out commit title, and every anchor back-ref to a
  now-gone per-sub-step section gets re-pointed — real work,
  done as part of the squash.
- **Keep separate (N + 1 commits)** when the decomposition is
  itself informative (different conceptual stages, design
  progression worth showing in `git log`). Used on
  `0.41.1-6.7` (8 sub-sub-step commits + 1 close-out commit).
  Each section keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref — no
  consolidation churn.

Pick at close-out, not at cycle start. No firm default — weigh
the squash's chores-consolidation cost against a cleaner
`git log`; that cost biases toward keeping separate unless the
decomposition genuinely isn't informative.

**`CLAUDE.md` changes:**
1. **`### Chores section headers`** — rewritten: a section header is now *exactly the commit title* (`<type>: <desc> (<version>)`); provisional during the work, synced to the final title — and every anchor back-ref to it updated — as the last edit before `vc-x1 push`. Notes that existing chores-01..09 sections keep their old `## Description (X.Y.Z)` headers (grandfathered); convention applies going forward; `0.48.2` converts the `0.48.1` section as the worked example.
2. **`### Chores: scannable bullets…` → `### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record`** — a chores section = a `Commits:` line + intro paragraph + `###` design subsections, and **no per-file edit list**; that list lives in the commit message body, which is the source of truth for the mechanical record. Chores owns the narrative/design; they cross-link, neither restates.
3. **NEW `### Chores commit references`** — the `Commits: [N],[M]` first-line convention; `# References` definition form `[N]: https://…/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"` (URL = clickable destination via short SHA; full 40-hex SHA in the title slot — host-agnostic, `git show`-able, scrape-able by external tooling); GitLab's `/-/` noted; backfilled at the *next* cycle's start; the single newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less (fine — the commit is the record).
4. **`### Pre-commit checklist`** — added a cycle-*start* step (backfill the previous section's `Commits:` ref); reworded item 8 (add a chores *section*, header = provisional commit title, intro + `###` subsections, no edit list); new item 9 (sync header → final commit title + fix anchor back-refs); old #9 → #10.
5. **`### App-repo body`** (Commit Message Style) — flipped: the commit body bullet list **is the source of truth** for the cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries narrative/design, not a copy.
6. **`### Cycle close-out — squash or keep separate?`** — dropped "default is squash"; added the squash *cost* (per-sub-step chores sections collapse into one, header becomes the close-out commit title, anchor back-refs re-pointed); "no firm default — that cost biases toward keeping separate unless the decomposition genuinely isn't informative."
7. **`### Commit-first review model`** step 6 — squashing a follow-up that changes the title now also moves the chores header + its anchor back-refs.

input.new_string:
## docs: por-dual capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1)

Commits: [1]

Docs-only follow-on to the 0.48.0 close-out (same shape as
the 0.42.1 docs cycle). Records the por/dual parity +
bidirectional-conversion goal as a forward-looking design
stub, and clears stale `init-clone-refactor` verbiage now
that the branch has landed in `main` (rebased at 0.42.0-4.7)
and been deleted local + remote. No code change.

## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)

Move the per-file edit list out of `chores-NN.md` sections
into the commit message body — git becomes the source of
truth for what each commit changed mechanically. A chores
section now keeps only the narrative + `###` design
subsections, takes its header from the commit title, and
cites its commit(s) via a `Commits:` ref. CLAUDE.md and
`notes/README.md` codify it; the `0.48.1` section above is
converted as the worked example, this section is born in the
new shape (its own `Commits:` ref backfilled at the next
cycle's start).

### Why git owns the edit record

- **DRY.** The old setup had the commit body mirror a chores
  `### Edits` list — the same content twice, which drifts.
  Git's copy is immutable, `git show`-able, commit-scoped; it
  wins as canonical. Chores keeps what git is bad at — the
  why.
- **`Commits:` ref shape.** Markdown reference-link
  definitions put the destination first, so to keep `[N]`
  clickable the URL is the destination
  (`…/commit/<12-hex>` — short, and GitHub / GitLab resolve a
  unique prefix), and the full 40-hex SHA rides in the title
  slot: host-agnostic, unambiguous, and present in the raw
  markdown for external tooling (a database, say) to scrape.
- **Backfill timing.** A commit's URL/SHA don't exist when
  its section is written, so `Commits:` is filled in at the
  *next* cycle's start — the newest section is briefly
  ref-less, which is fine (the commit itself is the record).

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/3f176b45235c "3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e"

new shape (its own `Commits:` ref backfilled at the next
cycle's start).

### Why git owns the edit record

- **DRY.** The old setup had the commit body mirror a chores
  `### Edits` list — the same content twice, which drifts.
  Git's copy is immutable, `git show`-able, commit-scoped; it
  wins as canonical. Chores keeps what git is bad at — the
  why.
- **`Commits:` ref shape.** Markdown reference-link
  definitions put the destination first, so to keep `[N]`
  clickable the URL is the destination
  (`…/commit/<12-hex>` — short, and GitHub / GitLab resolve a
  unique prefix), and the full 40-hex SHA rides in the title
  slot: host-agnostic, unambiguous, and present in the raw
  markdown for external tooling (a database, say) to scrape.
- **Backfill timing.** A commit's URL/SHA don't exist when
  its section is written, so `Commits:` is filled in at the
  *next* cycle's start — the newest section is briefly
  ref-less, which is fine (the commit itself is the record).

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/3f176b45235c "3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

App architecture — module map, the CLI-args / `Context`+`Params`
split, the subcommand model, and the in-flight migrations — lives
at the repo root in [`../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ARCHITECTURE.md).

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.
