input.old_string:
### Chores section headers

A chores section header is **exactly the commit title** it
records — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)):

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
```

The header is provisional while the work is in progress; the
*last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it to the final commit
title — and updates every markdown anchor back-reference to it
(`[N]: /notes/chores-NN.md#the-old-anchor` lines in `todo.md`,
`ARCHITECTURE.md`, sibling chores files, the file itself; see
[Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links) for the slug
algorithm). A dangling `#anchor` is the failure mode — the
pre-commit checklist catches it, and a future `vc-x1
validate-repo` should too.

Existing sections (most of chores-01..09) predate this and keep
their old `## Description (X.Y.Z)` headers; the convention
applies going forward — `0.48.2` converts the `0.48.1` section
as the worked example.
input.new_string:
### Headings and entries that record a commit

A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)).
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6) [3]`. For a multi-step cycle the `## Done` entry uses
the close-out commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
example.

+### Headings and entries that record a commit
+
+A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
+commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
+reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
+title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
+commit gets (see [Commit Message Style](#commit-message-style)).
+E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
+(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
+(0.48.0-6) [3]`. For a multi-step cycle the `## Done` entry uses
+the close-out commit's title.
+
+This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
+`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
+inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
+Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
+default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
+line a record up with its commit.
+
+A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
+progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
+`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
+commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
+for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
+dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
+(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
+
+Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
+the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
+free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
+converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
+example.
+
+### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
+
+A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
+header — see below), a short intro paragraph (what changed and
+*why*, a notch above file-list granularity), and any `###`
+design subsections. It does **not** carry a per-file edit list —
+that lives in the commit message body, which is the source of
+truth for "what changed mechanically" (immutable, `git
+show`-able, naturally scoped to the commit). The chores section
+is the source of truth for the design thinking; the two
+cross-link, neither restates the other.
+
+When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
+hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
+own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
+wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
+change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
+with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
+normal ref direction).
+
+**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
+to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
+drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
+narrative; `Commits:` links them.
+
+### Chores commit references
+
+The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
+line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
+
+```
+## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
+
+Commits: [3]
+
+<intro paragraph...>
+```
+
+`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
+[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering));
+multiple commits are `Commits: [3],[5]`. The `# References`
+definition puts the **commit URL** as the destination (so `[N]`
+renders as a clickable link), with the **full 40-hex SHA** in
+the title slot:
+
+```
+[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
+```
+
+- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
+  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
+  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
+- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
+  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
+  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
+  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
+
+**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
+written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
+change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
+the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
+newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
+commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
+finds it.
+At cycle *start* (before the version bump): backfill the
+previous chores section's `Commits:` ref with the just-pushed
+commit's URL + full SHA — see
+[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references).
+
-8. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change

+9. Sync the chores section header to the **final** commit title
+   and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
+10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new
+    flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
-   `jj describe @-` if the title needs to change).
+   `jj describe @-` — plus the chores section header and its
+   anchor back-refs — if the title needs to change).

content.text:
Got it — and yes, makes sense: the rule isn't "every heading is a commit title," it's "**a heading/entry that *records* a commit uses that commit's exact title**" — chores `##` cycle sections, `todo.md > ## Done` entries, and the `[N]` anchors derived from them; *not* organizational headings (`## Todo`, `# References`, …) or design `###` subsections. Exactness as the strong default, not absolute. Applied:

- **`CLAUDE.md`** — `### Chores section headers` → `### Headings and entries that record a commit`, generalized to cover Done entries + refs, with the "organizational/design headings excluded" carve-out, the "exact match is the strong default, nothing absolute" note, the multi-step → close-out-title rule, and a line that a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should verify the recorded title matches the commit. Grandfathering note now covers Done entries too.
- **`notes/chores-09.md` (0.48.2 section intro)** — "takes its header from the commit title" → "when it records a commit, as does a `todo.md > ## Done` entry, takes its header verbatim from that commit's title (organizational headings and design subsections don't)"; "the 0.48.1 section here and its `todo.md > ## Done` entry are converted as the worked example."
- **`notes/todo.md` Done entries** — now exact commit titles: `- docs: por-dual capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1) [92]` and `- docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2) [93]`. (`[92]`/`[93]` anchors already point at the right sections.)

(Diff above. Pre-`0.48.1` Done entries left as-is — grandfathered.)

**Reminder:** the `0.48.2` chores heading / Done entry / `[93]` anchor / that section's own anchor all use the *provisional* title `docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)`; if we pick a different final title at push, all four update together — the convention demonstrating itself.

**Other thoughts / open questions:**

1. **Multi-step Done entries get terser.** Under "exact close-out commit title," a future cycle's Done entry becomes e.g. `chore: close <X> cycle (0.49.0)` rather than today's descriptive style (`Migration A sweep — subcommand-layer ports: symlink/clone/… (0.48.0)`). The detail moves to the chores sections + per-step commit bodies, and the `[N]` link takes you there. I lean accept it (consistency > the at-a-glance Done blurb), but it's worth a deliberate nod — it'll bite first at `0.49.0` close-out. Alternative: give multi-step Done entries writer's-judgment leeway. Your call (now or at 0.49.0).
2. **`validate-repo` extension** — I put the "should verify recorded title matches the commit" line in CLAUDE.md. There's an existing `vc-x1 validate-repo` todo item; want me to fold a sub-bullet into it now (this cycle), or leave it for whoever picks `validate-repo` up?
3. **Anchor cosmetics** — commit-title headings carry `:` (from `<type>:`) and sometimes `→`/`—`; the GitHub slug drops them in place, so anchors get double-hyphens (`docs-por-dual-capture--icr-cleanup-0481`). Works fine — the repo already has `b--squash`-style anchors — just flagging it's expected, not a bug.

Anything else, or shall I move to assembling the `0.48.2` commit (run cargo, sync the provisional heading if you want a different title, propose title/body)?

content: Working through the 0.48.x docs cycles toward a Migration B sweep; 0.48.1 is pushed, and 0.48.2 (making git the source of truth for commit edit lists, plus the chores/commit-title convention) is edited but not committed. Next: confirm decisions on multi-step Done entries and validate-repo, then assemble and push the 0.48.2 commit.

1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## Memory
34	
35	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
36	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
37	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
38	and it loses on discoverability:
39	
40	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
41	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
42	  (human or bot).
43	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
44	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
45	
46	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
47	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
48	
49	## Speculation marker
50	
51	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
52	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
53	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
54	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
55	tell the measured from the inferred.
56	
57	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
58	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
59	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
60	in the written record.
61	
62	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
63	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
64	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
65	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
66	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
67	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
68	
69	## Committing
70	
71	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
72	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
73	visible at the start of the command.
74	
75	### App repo
76	```
77	jj commit -m \
78	"title" \
79	-m "body
80	
81	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
82	-R .
83	```
84	
85	### Bot session repo
86	```
87	jj commit -m \
88	"title" \
89	-m "body
90	
91	ochid: /<changeID>" \
92	-R .claude
93	```
94	
95	## jj Basics
96	
97	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
98	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
99	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
100	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
101	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
102	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
103	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
104	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
105	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
106	  session because session data updates continuously.
107	
108	## Commit Message Style
109	
110	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
111	a version suffix:
112	
113	```
114	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
115	```
116	
117	- **Title**: ≤50 chars (the "50" of the git 50/72 rule), a short
118	  summary of *what* changed. The version suffix counts toward the
119	  50 — the descriptive part gets whatever's left after `<type>: `
120	  and ` (<version>)`, so favor terse phrasings (`port X to Context`
121	  over `X → Context + XParams`) when the names run long. Common
122	  types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
123	- **Body line width**: wrap every body line at ≤72 cols (the "72"
124	  of the 50/72 rule); bullet continuations indent two spaces.
125	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
126	  terse bullet list — one bullet per file changed, the file plus a
127	  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). This list
128	  **is the source of truth** for the cycle's mechanical change
129	  record; the chores section carries the narrative + design, not a
130	  copy of it. Keep bullets terse — a scannable index, not prose;
131	  promote any "why" to a chores `###` subsection.
132	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
133	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
134	  in-session work, not code changes.
135	- Examples:
136	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
137	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
138	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
139	
140	## Pre-commit Requirements
141	
142	### User approval
143	
144	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
145	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
146	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
147	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
148	
149	### Review before proposing the commit block
150	
151	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
152	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
153	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
154	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
155	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
156	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
157	
158	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
159	review pause before its commit block appears.
160	
161	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
162	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
163	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
164	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
165	step before starting the next; don't ask.
166	
167	### Per-file review checkpoints
168	
169	The "review before commit block" rule applies to in-progress work
170	too, at finer grain. After each file edit, STOP, summarize what
171	changed, and wait for explicit go-ahead before touching the next
172	file or running follow-on commands (cargo test/install, dogfood
173	invocations, `vc-x1 …`, etc.).
174	
175	**Exceptions** — both ride along with the change they accompany,
176	no separate review pause:
177	
178	- **Refactors where code is moving across files** — e.g.
179	  lifting helpers into a new module and updating the original
180	  file's imports in the same change. Reviewing each half in
181	  isolation isn't useful.
182	- **`Cargo.toml` version bumps** that go with a specific code
183	  change.
184	
185	**Why:** the user reviews diffs as work lands. Chaining multiple
186	file edits without pausing forces them to untangle cumulative
187	state instead of inspecting each step in isolation.
188	
189	**How to apply:**
190	
191	- "continue", "go", "do it", "lg" approves *the next unit
192	  only*, not all remaining units of a multi-file plan. A green
193	  light to proceed with a *direction* is not a green light to

=== CLAUDE.md Chores commit references section ===
### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [3]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering));
multiple commits are `Commits: [3],[5]`. The `# References`
definition puts the **commit URL** as the destination (so `[N]`
renders as a clickable link), with the **full 40-hex SHA** in
the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

### Pre-commit checklist

content.text:
Looks good — `[[1]]` rendering as `[`+clickable-`1`+`]` is right, and yes, much clearer than a bare `1`. CommonMark/GitHub/VS Code all parse `[[1]]` as a literal `[`, the shortcut-ref `[1]`, and a literal `]` — the inner `[1]` still resolves against the `[1]:` definition. (Only caveat: a viewer that treats `[[…]]` as wiki-link syntax — Obsidian-style — would mangle it; none of the project's renderers do, so it's fine.) Codified.

Applied:
- **`CLAUDE.md` `### Chores commit references`** — example now `Commits: [[3]]`; explanation added (double-bracket so the brackets render; `[[3]],[[5]]` for several; the inner `[3]` resolves against `[3]:`). (Your `chores-09.md` `Commits: [[1]]` edit lines up with it.)
- **`notes/todo.md` `validate-repo` item** — folded in: "…plus chores↔commit consistency — every `[N]:` anchor resolves, and each chores `##` section's recorded title matches its `Commits:` commit's title…". (No new ref — the design's in CLAUDE.md.)
- **Multi-step Done entries** — settled (accept the terser close-out-commit-title form); no change needed, the rule's already in `### Headings and entries that record a commit`.

Two things back to you:

1. **Scope of `[[N]]`** — definitely for `Commits:` lines. Want it as the *going-forward* style for `[N]` citations generally too (the new-shape `## Done` entries, todo-item trailing refs, chores cross-refs)? It'd make `- docs: por-dual capture + icr cleanup (0.48.1) [[92]]` and `…worked example. [[80]],[[88]]` — same "brackets visible" win everywhere. Existing citations stay grandfathered. If yes, I'll flip the two `0.48.2` Done entries + the example in `### Headings and entries that record a commit` to `[[N]]` and note it in CLAUDE.md; if you'd rather keep it scoped to `Commits:`, leave them as `[N]`.

2. **Dup todo item** — there's a stray one-liner `1. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?` (item ~#5 in `## Todo`) alongside the detailed `validate-repo` item I just edited. Drop the one-liner as redundant?

Otherwise the `0.48.2` set is: `Cargo.toml`/`.lock` → `0.48.2`; `CLAUDE.md` (the convention); `notes/chores-09.md` (0.48.1 reshaped + 0.48.2 born-new + `# References`); `notes/README.md` (commit-ref note); `notes/todo.md` (Done entries + `[92]`/`[93]` + validate-repo). Nothing committed. Your call on 1 & 2, then I'll do final assembly.

content.text:
On the GitHub test — I'd skip it. I'm ~90% sure GitHub renders `[[27]]` as `[`+link+`]` with the link working (it follows CommonMark, where the inner `[27]` is a valid shortcut reference link). But you've *already* learned the thing that matters: `[[N]]` isn't universally safe — Zed breaks the inner link. A throwaway commit to confirm GitHub wouldn't change the conclusion, and it'd publish a scratch commit to GitHub's cache/index just for a rendering check. Not worth it.

So the real decision is: `[[N]]` (visible brackets, but link-fragile across viewers) vs plain `[N]` (no visible brackets, but links work in *every* CommonMark renderer). My recommendation: **go back to plain `[N]`.**

- Robustness wins for durable docs. A notes convention that silently breaks links in one of the two editors you use — and that we'd be *guessing* about for GitHub and any future viewer — is a bad trade for a marginal clarity gain.
- "Brackets disappear when rendered" is the standard, already-lived-with behavior of markdown reference links — the existing notes are full of `[80],[88]`-style trailing refs that render as bare clickable numbers. `Commits: 1` reads fine in context (the `Commits:` label + the bottom `# References` section make it obvious it's a ref); it's not worth fragility to dress it up.
- The *only* robust way to get visible brackets is the full-reference form `[\[1\]][1]` (link text `\[1\]` → renders `[1]`, reference label `1` → resolves against `[1]:`). It works everywhere — but it's noticeably uglier in the source (`Commits: [\[1\]][1], [\[3\]][3]`), and I'd not. Not worth it.

If you agree, I'll: revert the `[[…]]` edits (your `chores-09.md` `Commits: [[1]]`, your `todo.md` `[[27]]` / `[[28]],[[29]]`, my CLAUDE.md `[[3]]` example) back to single brackets, and add a one-liner to CLAUDE.md's `### Chores commit references` noting that the citation renders without surrounding brackets (expected; the `Commits:` label disambiguates) — so future-me doesn't re-litigate this.

(Counter-position, if you'd rather: keep `[[N]]` and accept Zed's broken link as a local-editor quirk, betting that GitHub — the canonical public view — handles it. Defensible if you weight GitHub-rendering over Zed-editing. But I'd take the robust option.)

Your call — revert to `[N]`, or keep `[[N]]`?

Ha — so the Zed "broken link" was a phantom: Zed's markdown *preview* just doesn't navigate **any** links (single `[N]` or `[[N]]` alike — it underlines/colors them but they're not clickable). So `[[N]]` isn't worse than `[N]` anywhere; it's strictly "visible brackets where the renderer supports clickable links (GitHub, VS Code), neutral where it doesn't (Zed preview)." The only real residual caveat is an Obsidian-style renderer that treats `[[…]]` as wiki-link syntax — and none of the project's viewers do that.

Given the downside evaporated and you like the brackets: I say **keep `[[N]]`**. It's a small, harmless, you-prefer-it improvement.

Scope, if you agree:
- **`Commits:` lines** — `[[N]]` (already codified in CLAUDE.md's `### Chores commit references`; your `chores-09.md` `Commits: [[1]]` matches).
- **Going-forward citations** generally — new `## Done` entries, new todo items, chores cross-refs use `[[N]]`. So I'd flip the two `0.48.2` Done entries → `[[92]]` / `[[93]]`, the example in `### Headings and entries that record a commit` → `[[3]]`, and update `notes/README.md`'s citation examples (`[[1]]`, `[[2]],[[3]]`) + CLAUDE.md's `### Notes references` line.
- **`todo.md`** — it's small and actively maintained, so I'd full-retrofit it to `[[N]]` now (your `[[27]]` / `[[28]],[[29]]` test edits + the rest), so it's uniform rather than mixed. The `# References` *definitions* stay `[N]:` (only the citations get the doubled brackets — I'll be careful not to touch the `[N]:` lines).
- **`chores-01..09`** — grandfathered (big, stable; not worth the churn). Existing `[N]` citations there stay as-is.

Confirm that scope and I'll do it as part of `0.48.2`. (Or, if "more trouble than it's worth" wins after all, say so and I'll revert the `[[…]]` edits — yours in `chores-09.md`/`todo.md` and my `[[3]]` in CLAUDE.md — back to `[N]`, and add a CLAUDE.md one-liner that bracketless rendering is expected.)

content.text:
On "universal" — I'd say **no, scope it to reference-style citations** (`[[N]]`, `[[N]],[[M]]`), and leave inline links (`[text](url)`, `[text](#anchor)`) alone. Reasoning:

- The "brackets disappear when rendered" problem only hits **reference citations** whose text is a bare number — `[27]` renders as `27`, which doesn't *read* as "a reference to something" (could be a count, a version fragment, anything). Doubling fixes exactly that.
- An inline link's text is a descriptive phrase — `[the Overview intro](url)` renders as `the Overview intro`, link-styled. It already reads as a link; wrapping it → `[the Overview intro]` just clutters prose for ~no gain.
- And in *raw source* (no preview), inline/anchor links already show their brackets (`[the thing](url)`); only reference *citations* lose bracket-ness in the rendered view (source `[N]` → rendered `N`). So the doubling targets the one form that actually drops brackets.

So the convention I'd codify: **reference-style numeric citations are double-bracketed (`[[N]]`) so they render as `[27]`, not `27`; `[N]:` definitions and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)` links are unchanged.**

If you agree with that scope, I'll do:
- **`todo.md`** — full-retrofit every `[N]` / `[N],[M]` citation → `[[N]]` (the `[N]:` definition lines and any inline links untouched).
- **`0.48.2` Done entries** → `[[92]]` / `[[93]]`; the example in `### Headings and entries that record a commit` → `[[3]]`.
- **`notes/README.md`** "Todo format" examples → `[[1]]`, `[[2]],[[3]]`; **CLAUDE.md** `### Notes references` line to match; the scoping note added (citations doubled, definitions/inline links not). (`### Chores commit references` already shows `Commits: [[3]]`.)
- **`chores-01..09`** — grandfathered, untouched.

Confirm the scope (citations only, not inline links) and I'll run the retrofit as part of `0.48.2`. Or if you really want it everywhere, I'll do that — but I'd advise against, per the above.

file.content:
Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

input.old_string:
Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
input.new_string:
Reference citations are **double-bracketed** — `[[1]]`, or
`[[2]],[[3]]` for several — so the brackets show when rendered
(a bare `[1]` renders as just `1`, which doesn't read as a
reference). Separate multiple with a comma: `[[2]],[[3]]`, not
`[2,3]` (a single ref key — won't resolve) or `[[2]][[3]]`. The
`[N]:` definition lines and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed (their brackets already show in
source, and the link text reads as a link when rendered).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

App architecture — module map, the CLI-args / `Context`+`Params`
split, the subcommand model, and the in-flight migrations — lives
at the repo root in [`../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ARCHITECTURE.md).

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

lines: work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
-Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
-In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
-is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).
+`[[2]],[[3]]` for several — so the brackets show when rendered
+(a bare `[1]` renders as just `1`, which doesn't read as a
+reference). Separate multiple with a comma: `[[2]],[[3]]`, not
+`[2,3]` (a single ref key — won't resolve) or `[[2]][[3]]`. The
+links stay single-bracketed (their brackets already show in
+source, and the link text reads as a link when rendered).

input.command: awk '/^### Why git owns the edit record$/,/^# References$/' notes/chores-09.md

content.content:
### Why git owns the edit record

- **DRY.** The old setup had the commit body mirror a chores
  `### Edits` list — the same content twice, which drifts.
  Git's copy is immutable, `git show`-able, commit-scoped; it
  wins as canonical. Chores keeps what git is bad at — the
  why.
- **`Commits:` ref shape.** Markdown reference-link
  definitions put the destination first, so to keep `[N]`
  clickable the URL is the destination
  (`…/commit/<12-hex>` — short, and GitHub / GitLab resolve a
  unique prefix), and the full 40-hex SHA rides in the title
  slot: host-agnostic, unambiguous, and present in the raw
  markdown for external tooling (a database, say) to scrape.
- **Backfill timing.** A commit's URL/SHA don't exist when
  its section is written, so `Commits:` is filled in at the
  *next* cycle's start — the newest section is briefly
  ref-less, which is fine (the commit itself is the record).

# References

Hmm — let me push back gently, because I think the file's actually mostly OK and the fix is small. I grepped every `[N]` in `chores-09.md`:

- **Lines 38–40, 88, 149, 180, 314–315, 683, 743, 817, 973–974** — every one of those `[N]` mentions is inside a **code span** (`` `[72]` ``, `` `[76]→[78]` ``, `` `[86]` ``, …). They're in the grandfathered `### Edits` lists, and they're not citations — they're literal text *quoting* a ref-key from **another file** ("added a `[86]` ref *to todo.md*", "`[72]→[76]` renumbering *in todo.md/chores-08*"). Code spans never become links, so these render as monospace `[72]` text regardless — which is exactly what's intended. Adding `[72]:`…`[88]:` definitions to `chores-09`'s `# References` wouldn't do anything for them (code spans don't resolve refs), and conceptually they're *not* `chores-09`-local refs anyway.
- **Line 114** — the one genuine exception: `Design references stay at chores-07 [76]:` has a *bare* `[76]` (not in a code span). With no `[76]:` def in `chores-09` it renders as literal `[76]` text — so it's not "broken," just inconsistent with the rest of the file (every other `[N]` here is code-spanned).
- **Line 978 / 1029** — `Commits: [[1]]` ↔ `[1]: …` — the real citation, resolves. ✓
- **Line 1022** — `` `[[N]]` (not `[N]`) … `[27]` `` — code-span examples *describing the convention*, not citations. ✓

So `chores-09`'s `# References` having just `[1]:` is correct — that's the only thing `chores-09` actually cites (the `Commits:` ref). It's not missing 70+ defs; it's missing zero (well, modulo line 114).

**Proposed fix** (`0.48.3`, small):
- Line 114: wrap `[76]` in a code span (`` `[76]` ``) so it matches the file's pervasive style — it's "quoting the `[76]` ref that `todo.md` tracks", same as every other `[N]` in the file.
- Add a one-liner to `notes/README.md`'s `## Reference numbering` (and/or a note in `chores-09`'s `# References`): a `[N]` written *inside a code span* is a quoted ref-key, not a citation — needs no definition; it may belong to another file's namespace.

That's it — a code-span wrap + a clarifying note. Want me to do that as `0.48.3`?

(If you genuinely want every `[72]`…`[88]` mention in `chores-09`'s `### Edits` lists turned into working links, that's a bigger and, I'd argue, wrong move — it means un-code-spanning historical edit descriptions and pointing them at other files' chores sections, while the `### Edits` convention is being sunset anyway. I'd not. But say the word if that's what you meant.)

- A **citation** (`[N]` intended to link to a target) should work or be deleted. ✓

Under the 0.48.2 convention, this is exactly the kind of thing that belongs in the commit body, not the chores narrative. Deleting the `[N]` parts (or whole clauses) loses nothing — the commit bodies have the specifics. Sites: lines 88, 149, 180, 314–315, 683, 743, 817, 973–974 (~7 spots).

Scope is `chores-09` only — `chores-01..08` have no `# References` section, so there's no incompleteness optic to fix; their similar `### Edits` `[N]` mentions stay grandfathered, untouched. Add a one-liner to `notes/README.md` (and maybe to `chores-09`'s `# References`) clarifying the principle — citation vs quoted ref-key as data — so this question doesn't re-litigate.

[77]: /notes/chores-07.md#substep-protocol-formalization-0420-45

And is valid in done.md, they could be literally copy and pasted
from done into chores-09.md and they'd work. But I also would
say that the number assigned to a link is purely valid only in
the that particular, there could be many [77]'s in the repo. So
basically these should be treated as "footnotes" which are numbered
and only relevant to the "page" they are on. So when moved they
should be renumbered and done.md can continue to use [77] but
in chores-09.md it would definitely not be [77].

Thoughts, do push back if you think I'm wrong!

content.text:
Not pushing back — you're right, and the framing is sharper than mine. Ref numbers as **footnotes** is exactly the right model:

- A `[N]` is a *file-local slot* — its only meaning is "the Nth entry in *this* file's `# References`." Two files' `[N]`s are unrelated slots that happen to share a digit. (notes/README.md already says "reference numbers are scoped to that file" — your framing makes the consequence explicit.)
- Therefore: writing `[77]` in `chores-09` to mean "todo.md's ref 77" is a category error — `chores-09`'s namespace doesn't have a `[77]` and shouldn't. If `chores-09` ever wants to *cite* the target todo.md's `[77]` points at, it picks its own next-available number (e.g. `[2]`) and defines it locally — coincidence with todo's `[77]` is just coincidence.

So those `### Edits` mentions of `[77]` / `[81]` / `[84]` / etc. in `chores-09` aren't just vestigial — they're using **foreign-namespace numbers as if they were local**, which is the same category of mistake as your earlier "looks like a ref but doesn't act like one." Deleting them is the right move under the principle, not just a polish.

The one nuance I'd preserve — and this is a *refinement* of your principle, not a counter:

- A **citation** (bare `[N]` / `[[N]]` parsed as a markdown ref-link) is file-local — never use another file's number.
- A **literal identifier** (`` `[N]` `` in a code span — monospace text, doesn't parse as a ref) is just data, like quoting a variable name. The renumbering description on line 38-40 (`` `[72]→[76]` `` in `### What needed real work`) is *naming* historical tokens from todo.md's namespace at the time of the rebase. It's not a citation; it's narrating a renumbering event, where the numbers *are* the content. Code-spans correctly say "literal token, not a ref into this file." Keep that one.

That tightens the `0.48.3` cleanup to:
- **Delete** vestigial `[N]` mentions in `chores-09`'s `### Edits` lists (~7 sites): they were using foreign-namespace numbers as if local. Reword clauses; the commit bodies carry the specifics for anyone who needs them.
- **Code-span** line 114's bare `[76]` (literal identifier, matches the survivors).
- **Keep** lines 38–40 (the renumbering description; substantive narration of historical tokens, correctly code-spanned).
- **Codify** in `notes/README.md`'s `## Reference numbering`: the footnote framing — file-local slots, citations never reuse another file's number; a `` `[N]` `` in a code span is a literal identifier (data), not a citation; if you want to cite a target a sibling file references, pick your own next-local number and define it.

Scope still `chores-09` only. (`chores-01..08` have similar vestigial mentions but no `# References` to make them glaring; their `### Edits` lists are being sunset by the 0.48.2 convention anyway. Touch them later or never.)

Sound? If so I'll start `0.48.3`.

content.text:
Y in principle — your "fix by renumbering" is the right move and honors the footnote framing. One subtlety I should surface before I do it, though, because it splits the cleanup into two categories:

**Many of the `### Edits` mentions would resolve self-referentially.** Pattern: a chores section X's `### Edits` mentions a ref-key that — in todo.md or done.md — points right back at X itself. Specifically:

- `[85]` ref added (in the 0.45.0 chores section) → `chores-09.md#architecture-doc-and-terminology-reconciliation-0450` = that same 0.45.0 section.
- `[86]` (in 0.46.0) → that 0.46.0 section.
- `[88]` (in 0.48.0) → that 0.48.0 section.
- `[79]` (in 0.42.0-4.7) → that section. `[81]` (in 0.42.0 close-out) → that section. `[84]` (in 0.43.0-0) → that section.

Renumbering these locally still works mechanically — `[N]: …#same-section-im-in` — but the resulting click is a no-op (you click and land where you already are). That's "working" only in the technical sense.

Cross-pointing mentions, on the other hand, *do* benefit from the fix:

- Line 114's `[76]` → chores-07's `--scope` enum refactor section. ✓
- `[80]` → `/ARCHITECTURE.md`. ✓
- `[82]` / `[83]` → `forks-multi-user.md` / `bot-data-formats.md`. ✓
- `[77]` / `[78]` → chores-07 sections. ✓

So I'd propose a small refinement of strict "fix all":

- **Cross-pointing mentions** → renumber locally + define in `chores-09`'s `# References`. Useful clicks.
- **Self-referential mentions** → delete (the click wouldn't add value; the section already *is* what the ref denotes).
- **Line 38–40** (renumbering narration) → leave code-spanned (literal historical tokens; `[72]`/`[73]`/`[74]` got renumbered away and have no stable current target anyway).

Specifically: line 973-974's mixed list ("received the five migrated entries + `[77]` `[78]` `[79]` `[81]` `[84]` refs") splits — `[77]`/`[78]` cross-point (chores-07), `[79]`/`[81]`/`[84]` self-reference (chores-09 sections); reword to keep the useful pointers and drop the self-refs.

Net result: ~6 cross-pointing fixes get chores-09-local `[2]..[7]` slots + defs; ~6 self-referential mentions get pruned. The principle is fully honored (every citation works, none are no-ops), and chores-09 stops carrying useless link-clicks.

y/n on this split? If y, I'll start `0.48.3`.

content.text:
Concrete numbers from the recon:

- **14 `### Edits` subsections** in chores-09 (lines 83, 143, 161, 309, 342, 370, 410, 450, 498, 593, 656, 711, 812, 961).
- **22 commit-recording sections** in chores-09 (plus one stub at line 506 that has no commit — `## Subcommand-layer architecture — moved to ARCHITECTURE.md`).
- **Commit titles + SHAs all looked up cleanly** via `jj log` — every section maps to a known commit. Headings range from minor renames (just add `chore:`/`refactor:` prefix) to substantial rewrites (`## InitParams implementation (0.44.0)` → `## refactor: introduce Context + InitParams (0.44.0)`).
- **~10 back-refs to cascade**: 4 in todo.md (`[85]`/`[86]`/`[87]`/`[88]`), 3 in done.md (`[79]`/`[81]`/`[84]`), 2 prose mentions in `init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` (lines 12, 121). `[89]`/`[90]` in todo.md point at design `###` sub-subsections inside chores-09 (not commit-recording), so they're unaffected.

So **option A** (full retro convert) = 22 heading renames + 14 `### Edits` deletions + 22 `Commits: [[N]]` first-lines + 22 `[N]:` defs in chores-09's `# References` (locally renumbered `[2]..[23]`) + ~10 back-ref updates + the line 114 fix + the `notes/README.md` footnote-framing note. Substantial chunk — call it ~45–60 min of careful editing — but every piece is mechanical and bounded; commit titles + SHAs already mapped.

**Option B** (delete `### Edits` only, leave headings) = 14 deletions + line 114 fix + footnote framing. ~15–20 min. Headings stay old-shape, no `Commits:` links, back-refs untouched.

My honest take: **A is the right call** — your principle is "references should work," and that extends naturally to "every chores section is paired with its commit via `Commits:`." It's the convention applied uniformly to one file (chores-09) so it stops being a half-converted artifact. The cost is real but tractable, and the commit-title/SHA lookups are already done. After this, chores-09 = uniformly new-shape, line 38-40 the only code-spanned historical narration, `# References` cleanly indexed with 22 commit refs.

B is the perfectly defensible "good enough" fallback if you'd rather not invest the time.

y on A? n → I do B.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Bulk-convert chores-09.md sections to the 0.48.2 new shape.

For each commit-recording section: rename heading to the exact commit
title, insert `Commits: [[N]]` first-line, delete the `### Edits`
subsection (if any).  Then patch line 114 (foreign-namespace `[76]` →
chores-09-local `[[24]]`), backfill 0.48.2's Commits ref, and add
`[2]..[24]` to the `# References` section.
"""
import re
import sys

PATH = 'notes/chores-09.md'

# (old_heading, new_heading, chores-09-local ref number, full git SHA)
SECTIONS = [
    ("## init-clone-refactor rebase landing (0.42.0-4.7)",
     "## chore: init-clone-refactor rebase landing (0.42.0-4.7)",
     2, "bdec8579c28b76989e52807a9e6bba93ba301c96"),
    ("## 0.42.0 close-out",
     "## chore: close 0.42.0 cycle at -4.7 (0.42.0)",
     3, "bb27daa86a078e2e06ebc56e0159e89829fb6356"),
    ("## Design notes: bot-data + multi-user updates (0.42.1)",
     "## docs: bot-data-formats + multi-user notes (0.42.1)",
     4, "7880bfe2700d8ce47946e5d9f6bf7e05e34de5c6"),
    ("## Test-module extraction (0.43.0)",
     "## chore: open 0.43.0 cycle (0.43.0-0)",
     5, "c1525175948c1a97118996bb0c997c37c81351ff"),
    ("## init test extraction (0.43.0-1)",
     "## refactor: extract init test module (0.43.0-1)",
     6, "bc97a768e6437279599311eb57ee3eb66b549295"),
    ("## push test extraction (0.43.0-2)",
     "## refactor: extract push test modules (0.43.0-2)",
     7, "29b2e9c73b2d9cb29b7b05f5389b02338b7dab43"),
    ("## sync test extraction (0.43.0-3)",
     "## refactor: extract sync test modules (0.43.0-3)",
     8, "fc7b518a07319a40b580e149c3900b1b7bae27ad"),
    ("## common test extraction (0.43.0-4)",
     "## refactor: extract common test module (0.43.0-4)",
     9, "3a0fa145fc694477180179f58b1fb71043d070a8"),
    ("## 0.43.0 close-out",
     "## chore: close 0.43.0 cycle (0.43.0)",
     10, "545c7c725b3143d6ed1e92b870e8370362eaafdf"),
    ("## InitParams implementation (0.44.0)",
     "## refactor: introduce Context + InitParams (0.44.0)",
     11, "be7f2502ddedbe6413212ef9df6bf93852c1a538"),
    ("## Architecture doc and terminology reconciliation (0.45.0)",
     "## docs: ARCHITECTURE.md + subcommand-layer naming (0.45.0)",
     12, "e966d65feef8b93791dcfef1f3c64a37865ffb32"),
    ("## finalize subcommand-layer migration (0.46.0)",
     "## refactor: finalize → Context + FinalizeParams (0.46.0)",
     13, "d02782e14eff44a969b45644bd893ec7bf8e5bb5"),
    ("## finalize Migration B — squash options_flags leaf (0.47.0)",
     "## refactor: finalize --squash → options_flags leaf (0.47.0)",
     14, "aad07a58e033226734465078966cbfdde7dc48c7"),
    ("## Migration A sweep: subcommand-layer ports (0.48.0)",
     "## chore: open 0.48.0 cycle — Migration A sweep (0.48.0-0)",
     15, "c4daeef0355187deac2f261572bc71ca3a0ef345"),
    ("## symlink → Context + SymlinkParams (0.48.0-1)",
     "## refactor: symlink → Context + SymlinkParams (0.48.0-1)",
     16, "6a5299ea624dbcbe87e115258656baf5d96010ac"),
    ("## clone → Context + CloneParams (0.48.0-2)",
     "## refactor: clone → Context + CloneParams (0.48.0-2)",
     17, "0529aa50c8777506d65232caa61a33f65d81d11a"),
    ("## sync → Context + SyncParams (0.48.0-3)",
     "## refactor: sync → Context + SyncParams (0.48.0-3)",
     18, "a2a30f7ed1c1c25cd164a4bcf0713f27737bb006"),
    ("## validate-desc → Context + ValidateDescParams (0.48.0-4)",
     "## refactor: port validate-desc to Context (0.48.0-4)",
     19, "aa0ee07c714448678651fab8a16b67fcc083ab8d"),
    ("## fix-desc → Context + FixDescParams (0.48.0-5)",
     "## refactor: port fix-desc to Context (0.48.0-5)",
     20, "88b0e5c3b114f799928d1d593aecdad46e1179cd"),
    ("## push → Context + PushParams (0.48.0-6)",
     "## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)",
     21, "c9d89386fb06876d1ee29e5cf370acc3c83caf66"),
    ("## 0.48.0 close-out",
     "## chore: close Migration A sweep cycle (0.48.0)",
     22, "e284505c346ca051e03d36889389e49229252904"),
]

OPEN_482_SHA = "79279d112791c12190a755b8aec9be1ec174ecb8"  # ref [23]

with open(PATH) as f:
    content = f.read()

# Step 1: rename headings, insert Commits: [[N]] line
errors = []
for old, new, n, sha in SECTIONS:
    needle = old + "\n\n"
    if content.count(needle) != 1:
        errors.append(f"pattern {old!r}\\n\\n count={content.count(needle)}")
        continue
    content = content.replace(needle, new + f"\n\nCommits: [[{n}]]\n\n", 1)

if errors:
    print("ERRORS:")
    for e in errors:
        print("  " + e)
    sys.exit(1)

# Step 2: delete ### Edits subsections (any variant — "### Edits",
# "### Edits (this commit)", "### Edits (this sub-step, …)" etc.).
# Each is the last subsection inside its `##` parent, so it ends at the
# next `## ` heading or EOF.  Consume leading blank line(s) too.
edits_count_before = len(re.findall(r"^### Edits", content, flags=re.M))
content = re.sub(
    r"\n*### Edits[^\n]*\n.*?(?=\n## |\Z)",
    "",
    content,
    flags=re.DOTALL
)
edits_count_after = len(re.findall(r"^### Edits", content, flags=re.M))
print(f"### Edits subsections: {edits_count_before} → {edits_count_after}")

# Step 3: backfill 0.48.2's Commits ref
old_482 = "## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)\n\n"
new_482 = "## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)\n\nCommits: [[23]]\n\n"
assert content.count(old_482) == 1, "0.48.2 heading not unique"
content = content.replace(old_482, new_482, 1)

# Step 4: line 114 fix — bare [76] → [[24]]
old_114 = "Design references stay\nat chores-07 [76]:"
new_114 = "Design references stay\nat chores-07 [[24]]:"
assert content.count(old_114) == 1, "line 114 not unique"
content = content.replace(old_114, new_114, 1)

# Step 5: extend # References with [2]..[24]
old_refs = '[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/3f176b45235c "3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e"\n'
new_lines = [old_refs]
for old, new, n, sha in SECTIONS:
    short = sha[:12]
    new_lines.append(f'[{n}]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/{short} "{sha}"\n')
new_lines.append(f'[23]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/{OPEN_482_SHA[:12]} "{OPEN_482_SHA}"\n')
new_lines.append(f'[24]: /notes/chores-07.md#--scope-enum-refactor-0420\n')
new_refs = "".join(new_lines)
assert content.count(old_refs) == 1, "[1] ref line not unique"
content = content.replace(old_refs, new_refs, 1)

with open(PATH, 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)

print("done.")

OPEN_482_SHA = "79279d112791c12190a755b8aec9be1ec174ecb8"  # ref [23]

with open(PATH) as f:
    content = f.read()

# Step 1: rename headings, insert Commits: [[N]] line
errors = []
for old, new, n, sha in SECTIONS:
    needle = old + "\n\n"
    if content.count(needle) != 1:
        errors.append(f"pattern {old!r}\\n\\n count={content.count(needle)}")
        continue
    content = content.replace(needle, new + f"\n\nCommits: [[{n}]]\n\n", 1)

if errors:
    print("ERRORS:")
    for e in errors:
        print("  " + e)
    sys.exit(1)

# Step 2: delete ### Edits subsections (any variant — "### Edits",
# "### Edits (this commit)", "### Edits (this sub-step, …)" etc.).
# Each is the last subsection inside its `##` parent, so it ends at the
# next `## ` heading or EOF.  Consume leading blank line(s) too.
edits_count_before = len(re.findall(r"^### Edits", content, flags=re.M))
content = re.sub(
    r"\n*### Edits[^\n]*\n.*?(?=\n## |\Z)",
    "",
    content,
    flags=re.DOTALL
)
edits_count_after = len(re.findall(r"^### Edits", content, flags=re.M))
print(f"### Edits subsections: {edits_count_before} → {edits_count_after}")

# Step 3: backfill 0.48.2's Commits ref
old_482 = "## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)\n\n"
new_482 = "## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)\n\nCommits: [[23]]\n\n"
assert content.count(old_482) == 1, "0.48.2 heading not unique"
content = content.replace(old_482, new_482, 1)

# Step 4: line 114 fix — bare [76] → [[24]]
old_114 = "Design references stay\nat chores-07 [76]:"
new_114 = "Design references stay\nat chores-07 [[24]]:"
assert content.count(old_114) == 1, "line 114 not unique"
content = content.replace(old_114, new_114, 1)

# Step 5: extend # References with [2]..[24]
old_refs = '[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/3f176b45235c "3f176b45235c4bc7da4b8de5b18a7f454c464d1e"\n'
new_lines = [old_refs]
for old, new, n, sha in SECTIONS:
    short = sha[:12]
    new_lines.append(f'[{n}]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/{short} "{sha}"\n')
new_lines.append(f'[23]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/{OPEN_482_SHA[:12]} "{OPEN_482_SHA}"\n')
new_lines.append(f'[24]: /notes/chores-07.md#--scope-enum-refactor-0420\n')
new_refs = "".join(new_lines)
assert content.count(old_refs) == 1, "[1] ref line not unique"
content = content.replace(old_refs, new_refs, 1)

with open(PATH, 'w') as f:
    f.write(content)

print("done.")

49	- `rslv-commit` cursor bookmark — handy as a navigation

146	## chore: open 0.43.0 cycle (0.43.0-0)
147	
148	Commits: [[5]]
149	
150	Multi-step cycle to extract `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (and
151	`mod integration_tests` where present) from oversized
152	production files into sibling-submodule layout. Pure
153	mechanical reshape — no behavior change, no API change.
154	Each test still reaches private items via
155	`use super::*;`.
156	
157	### Goals
158	
159	- Shrink production files so the actual code is easier
160	  to navigate (`init.rs` is 2576 lines today, ~1093 of
161	  which are tests).
162	- Establish a layout shape that future cycles can keep
163	  using (further splitting into `cli.rs` / `ops.rs` per
164	  the subcommand-layer architecture, etc.).
165	- Done as one cycle so the four files end up
166	  consistent rather than half-converted across multiple
167	  cycles.
168	
169	### Per-file shape
170	
171	Non-mod.rs layout (Rust 2018+ idiom): the production
172	file keeps its top-level path; a sibling directory holds
173	children. `src/X.rs` and `src/X/` coexist — Rust resolves
174	`mod foo;` declared in `src/X.rs` to `src/X/foo.rs`.
175	
176	```
177	src/X.rs               ← production code (unchanged path)
178	src/X/
179	  tests.rs             ← moved from #[cfg(test)] mod tests
180	  integration_tests.rs ← moved from #[cfg(test)] mod
181	                         integration_tests (push, sync)
182	```
183	
184	In `src/X.rs` after extraction:
185	
186	```rust
187	// ...production code...
188	
189	#[cfg(test)]
190	mod tests;
191	
192	#[cfg(test)]
193	mod integration_tests;  // only for push, sync
194	```
195	
196	In `src/X/tests.rs`:
197	
198	```rust
199	//! Unit tests for the X module.
200	
201	use super::*;
202	// ...test fns...
203	```
204	
205	**Why non-mod.rs.** The `mod.rs` style names every
206	module's entry point identically — fuzzy finders show a
207	column of `mod.rs` rows that the reader must
208	disambiguate by directory. Multiple `tests.rs` files
209	don't have the same pain (the file is leaf content; the
210	directory tells the reader which module's tests they
211	are). Modern Rust (`cargo new`, RFC 2126) defaults to

content.content:
## docs: chores edit list → commit message (0.48.2)

Commits: [[23]]

Move the per-file edit list out of `chores-NN.md` sections
into the commit message body — git becomes the source of
truth for what each commit changed mechanically. A chores
section now keeps only the narrative + `###` design
subsections, cites its commit(s) via a `Commits:` ref, and —
when it records a commit, as does a `todo.md > ## Done` entry —
takes its header verbatim from that commit's title
(organizational headings and design subsections don't).
CLAUDE.md and `notes/README.md` codify it; the `0.48.1`
section here and its `todo.md > ## Done` entry are converted
as the worked example, this section is born in the new shape
(its own `Commits:` ref backfilled at the next cycle's start).

### Why git owns the edit record

- **DRY.** The old setup had the commit body mirror a chores
  `### Edits` list — the same content twice, which drifts.
  Git's copy is immutable, `git show`-able, commit-scoped; it
  wins as canonical. Chores keeps what git is bad at — the
  why.
- **`Commits:` ref shape.** Markdown reference-link
  definitions put the destination first, so to keep the
  `[[N]]` citation clickable the `[N]:` definition's
  destination is the URL (`…/commit/<12-hex>` — short, and
  GitHub / GitLab resolve a unique prefix), and the full
  40-hex SHA rides in the title slot: host-agnostic,
  unambiguous, and present in the raw markdown for external
  tooling (a database, say) to scrape.
- **Backfill timing.** A commit's URL/SHA don't exist when
  its section is written, so `Commits:` is filled in at the
  *next* cycle's start — the newest section is briefly
  ref-less, which is fine (the commit itself is the record).
- **Doubled citations.** Reference *citations* are written
  `[[N]]` (not `[N]`) so the brackets render — `[27]` reads
  as a reference, `27` doesn't. `[N]:` definitions and inline
  links stay single-bracketed. `todo.md` is fully retrofitted;
  `chores-01..09` are grandfathered.

# References

input.new_string:
links stay single-bracketed. `todo.md` is fully retrofitted;
  `chores-01..08` grandfathered (chores-09 retrofitted in
  0.48.3).

## docs: chores-09 → new shape (0.48.3)

Retroactively convert every commit-recording section in
`chores-09.md` to the 0.48.2 convention: section header =
exact commit title; `Commits: [[N]]` first-line citing the
section's commit; `### Edits` subsections removed (the commit
body is the record). Line 114's bare `[76]` (a
foreign-namespace citation attempt) → a `chores-09`-local ref
to the chores-07 `--scope` design section. Lines 38–40's
renumbering description left code-spanned (literal historical
tokens, no stable current target). `notes/README.md` codifies
the footnote framing — ref numbers are file-local slots; a
`[N]` in a code span is a quoted identifier (data), not a
citation; cross-file pointing uses an inline link.

### Why retrofit one file

- **Surfaced by adding `# References`.** 0.48.2 gave
  `chores-09` a `# References` section; a reader then expects
  it to define the page's `[N]` mentions — but most were
  `todo.md`'s / `done.md`'s numbers written into `chores-09`
  prose, a category error. Half-implemented was the worst of
  both; either the page runs the convention fully or it
  doesn't.
- **Bounded.** One file, 21 historical commit-recording
  sections, ~10 back-refs (`todo.md` `[85]`–`[88]`, `done.md`
  `[79]`/`[81]`/`[84]`, two prose mentions in
  `init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`). `chores-01..08` have
  similar vestigials but no `# References` forcing the issue —
  they stay grandfathered (the convention sunsets their
  `### Edits` lists by attrition, not retrofit).

# References

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions taken
(see Resolution below).

## Resolution (2026-05-11)

`init-clone-refactor` landed into `main` at `0.42.0-4.7`
(see `notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`): `main`'s `0.42.0-0..-4.6` work was
rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
branch has since been deleted locally and remotely.

The "still deferred" / "still open" wording in the dated
sections below is superseded — the merge-direction question
is resolved (rebase-main-onto-branch), and the chores-NN
ordering follow-up survives only as a low-priority entry in
`notes/todo.md > ## Todo`. This file is retained as a
historical post-mortem; the recovery playbook and
diagnostic techniques remain reusable.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (resolved 2026-05-11)

The merge-direction call, once the recovered branch
gave a clean base to choose from:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Resolved: the "vice versa" topology — `main`'s
`0.42.0-0..-4.6` work rebased on top of the branch at
its `0.41.1` close-out tip, force-pushed at close-out
(`0.42.0-4.7`). See Resolution above and
`notes/chores-09.md > ## init-clone-refactor rebase
landing (0.42.0-4.7)`.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions (resolved — see Resolution above)

All resolved by the `0.42.0-4.7` rebase landing:

- **Merge direction** — settled: `main`'s `0.42.0-X`
  work rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1`
  close-out tip.
- **chores-NN ordering** — survives only as a
  low-priority entry in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo`.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0** — the real
  conflicts (`Cargo.toml`, `notes/todo.md`,
  `notes/chores-08.md`, CLAUDE.md, `src/init.rs`,
  `src/scope.rs`, `src/common.rs`) surfaced and were
  resolved during the cascade; see
  `notes/chores-09.md > ### What needed real work`.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

lines: rebased on top of the branch at its `0.41.1` close-out tip,
so the branch's commits are now ancestors of `main`. The
`0.42.0-0..-4.6` work rebased on top of the branch at
its `0.41.1` close-out tip, force-pushed at close-out

content.content:
## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

## Retiring Done entries

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely

Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]` — see below) never reuses another
file's number; to cite a target a sibling file references, pick
your own next-local slot and define it (the same target may carry
a different number in each file). A `[N]` *inside a code span*
(`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's a quoted identifier: literal
text naming a ref-key (often from another file's namespace), data,
not a citation; it needs no definition here. To point at a section
of another file from prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.

App architecture — module map, the CLI-args / `Context`+`Params`
split, the subcommand model, and the in-flight migrations — lives
at the repo root in [`../ARCHITECTURE.md`](../ARCHITECTURE.md).

In the future we I expect we may want to create a "notes"
database to better manage the information, TBD.

Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow, versioning, and code conventions live in
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Start there for:

- **Versioning during development** — single-step vs multi-step,
  `-N` pre-release suffixes, done-marker discipline.
- **Code Conventions** — doc comments on every file / fn / method,
  `// OK: …` justifications on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.
- **Commit-Push-Finalize Flow** — two-checkpoint per-step
  discipline with hard stop after finalize.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Todo items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. ` and the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them. Reorder or insert items without
renumbering, and reference an entry by its displayed number ("let's
work on #3"). The Done section keeps `-` bullets — items aren't
referenced by number once completed.

Reference citations are **double-bracketed** — `[[1]]`, or
`[[2]],[[3]]` for several — so the brackets show when rendered
(a bare `[1]` renders as just `1`, which doesn't read as a
reference). Separate multiple with a comma: `[[2]],[[3]]`, not
`[2,3]` (a single ref key — won't resolve) or `[[2]][[3]]`. The
`[N]:` definition lines and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed (their brackets already show in
source, and the link text reads as a link when rendered).

Examples:

# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z

## Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a
file-local `# References` section at the bottom. Reference numbers
are scoped to that file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in
`chores-01.md` are independent slots that may point at completely
different URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[`../CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

## Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently shipped
work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md` at two natural
beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) commit
  ships, decide which prior entries are no longer needed for nearby
  context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep before
  bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are file-local,
  so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer cited
  by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` / `## Done`. This
  frees the numbers for future reuse.

- **`# References` extended** to `[1]..[24]`: `[1]..[23]` are commit refs (`url-12hex "40hex"` form), `[24]` is the chores-07 design pointer for line 114's fixed `[[24]]` (was a bare foreign-namespace `[76]`).
